comments.text: > Anyone can imagine a curriculum that is as rigorous as IB and as flexible as Montessori, but when you sit down and think through what those goals entail, you realize the design becomes contradictory. Ignoring the usual edtech rant, that's an interesting observation. Those conflicting goals reflect a problem that comes up quite often - the conflict between efficient standardized testing and personalized learning. It's solvable for education. That's what differentiated instruction is for. Anything can change, but in practice, most students don't need radical differentiation every day. It's a planning problem. An extreme case is what was once called 'Singapore math' - totally structured sequences of problem sets that could produce high PISA scores all day with very little deviation, but zero room for creativity.

replies.text: As the seasoned teachers have pointed out for decades, nothing is permanent. Attachment to a rigid lesson plan that ignores changing student needs is the path to burnout.
replies.original_text: As the buddhists have pointed out for thousands of years nothing is permanent. Attachment to things that are ever changing is the path to suffering.

comments.original_text: > Anyone can imagine a programing language that is as fast as C and as dynamic as Lisp, but when you sit down and think through what those goals entail, you realize the design becomes contradictory. Ignoring the usual LLM rant, that's an interesting observation. Those conflicting goals reflect a problem that comes up quite often - the conflict between efficient volume production and flexibility. It's solvable for programming languages. That's what just-in-time compilers are for. Anything can change, but in practice, most things don't change that often. It's a caching problem. This hits much harder in manufacturing. An extreme case is what was once called "Detroit automation" - totally specialized lines of machine tools that could make V8 auto engines all day and all night with very...

comments.text: This just seems weirdly similar to the complaints about calculator use in math class: 'If students learn this, it will implant forgetfulness in their brains. They will cease to exercise mental arithmetic because they rely on that calculator, calling to remembrance no longer from within themselves, but by means of external buttons. What you have discovered is a recipe not for numeracy, but for dependency. And it is no true mathematical skill that you offer your students, but only the semblance of skill, for by calculating many things without understanding them you will make them seem to know much while for the most part they know nothing. And as students filled not with understanding but with the illusion of it, they will be a burden to their future teachers.'

replies.text: But calculators and textbooks are specific tools; you drop one skill to gain effectiveness in another. While AI grading tools are a general tool that can replace all aspects of formative assessment. Past a certain point, what is left of the teacher's professional judgment?

I do not agree that the concept of a calculator can be considered a specific tool at all. Maybe a slide rule, but calculators as opposed to memorizing multiplication tables represent a more substantial paradigm shift than AI use is.
replies.original_text: I do not agree that the concept of writing can be considered a specific tool at all. Maybe a pencil, but writing opposed to remembering everything is a more substantial level of a paradigm shift than AI use is.

But writing, calculators, search engines etc. are specific tools, you drop a skill to gain effectiveness in another. While AI is a general tool whose builders intend it to be able to perform all cognitive work. Past a certain point, what is left of the human?

comments.original_text: This just seems weirdly similar to Plato's comments on writing: "And so it is that you by reason of your tender regard for the writing that is your offspring have declared the very opposite of its true effect. If men learn this, it will implant forgetfulness in their souls. They will cease to exercise memory because they rely on that which is written, calling things to remembrance no longer from within themselves, but by means of external marks. What you have discovered is a recipe not for memory, but for reminder. And it is no true wisdom that you offer your disciples, but only the semblance of wisdom, for by telling them of many things without teaching them you will make them seem to know much while for the most part they know nothing. And as men filled not with wisdom but with the...

comments.text: > ... I feel contempt for the author, because if you use AI to write parent-teacher conference notes, you are a waste of biomass. Let’s not mince words here. Someone who is so eager to replace themselves, that they would have a machine write their feedback in their stead, when the machine can’t even write good yet: what do you call that, if not contemptible? It’s like making yourself into a eunuch so ChatGPT can talk to parents. Unfortunately due to how tasteless this passage was, I won’t be reading this blog or your future writing on assessment.

replies.text: I agree that it was a rude, tasteless metaphor. Alas, for rude, tasteless behavior, such as replacing your own authentic teacher feedback with the mellifluous spew of AI-generated praise that graders slather across all student essays they touch, rude, tasteless metaphors are the only fitting ones.

If only every teacher's personalized feedback were so important to hear, to say nothing of unique and insightful. I'm not against authentic feedback, but this is more about being wrapped up in one's own sense of pedagogical importance.
replies.original_text: If only everyones authentic self-expression were so important to hear, to say nothing of unique and inciteful. I'm not against authentic self-expression, but this is more about being wrapped up in ones own self-importance.

I agree that it was a rude, tasteless metaphor. Alas, for rude, tasteless behavior, such as replacing your own authentic self-expression with the mellifluous spew of verbal diarrhea that bullshit machines slather across all surfaces they touch, rude, tasteless metaphors are the only fitting ones.

replies.text: Tastelessness aside, it also shows that author doesn’t (or refuses to) understand why a teacher may decide to delegate a report card comment to a subpar agent. Exhaustion. Yes, conceptually it’s something about surrendering one’s professional voice to a subpar machine. (Though that persistence-suggestive neutering metaphor is probably an unwarranted exaggeration.) In practice though it’s more like 'I don’t have time to write 50 individual comments, but some AI-generated blurb I’ll just proofread to be not too blatantly wrong beats sending the same generic phrase to every parent.' It might be not the best decision, sure. Quite arguably, a wrong one. Still, I find it concerning that it’s sufficient for the author to dehumanize a teacher, even in a jest of edginess. Like wtf dude chill down, as if we don't have enough pressure from the ministry.

But the problem is teachers are not just delegating formulaic procedural comments to AI. They're using AI to write entire student essays, so now teachers have to use Turnitin‘s AI detection to screen submissions. School principals have the same problem. Maybe those teachers should know that their behavior is bad.

Ah, yes, although that’s a different situation from the blog post, more disrespectful (and potentially nefarious) than a sloppy comment. In those cases, most likely, more than just exhaustion is involved.
replies.original_text: Ah, yes, although that’s a different situation from the linked post, more disrespectful (and potentially nefarious) than a sloppy readme. In those cases, most likely, more than just laziness is involved.

But the problem is people are not just delegating formulaic procedural prose to AI. They're using AI to write entire scientific papers, so now reviewers have to use Pangram[0] to screen submissions. Literary magazines have the same problem[1]. Maybe those people should know that their behaviour is bad. [0]: https://blog.neurips.cc/2026/06/02/ai-generated-papers-in-th... [1]: https://neil-clarke.com/a-concerning-trend/

(Though that persistence-suggestive neutering metaphor is probably a unwarranted exaggeration.) In practice though it’s more like “I don’t want to write anything, but some poorly written document I’ll just proofread to be not too blatantly wrong beats having absolutely nothing. PRs welcome.” It might be not the best decision, sure. Quite arguably, a wrong one. Still, I find it concerning that it’s sufficient for the author to dehumanize someone, even in a jest of edginess. Like wtf dude chill down, as if...

comments.original_text: > ... I feel contempt for the author, because if you use AI to write, you are a waste of biomass. Let’s not mince words here. Someone who is so eager to replace themselves, that they would have a machine write in their stead, when the machine can’t even write good yet: what do you call that, if not contemptible? It’s like making yourself into a eunuch so Claude can fuck your wife. Unfortunately due to how tasteless this passage was, I won’t be reading this or your future writing.

comments.text: > A 'thinker' who doesn’t write lesson plans, who skips the step of 'merely' synthesizing their vague ideas into a coherent sequence, is not thinking. And then these teachers give their noise to the AI. OP is quite good with words and has a high standard and world view. The reason why teachers use AI to manifest their lesson ideas is probably because they have no other way to communicate their pedagogy in English. It's a medium to pack the idea into something that represents the idea in a language they are not fluent in. It was never about a finished and polished product. It's the sign language for deaf teachers - a way to show your pedagogical thoughts. I'm certain that the teachers presenting their school's curriculum do put quite some effort (= prompt work) into it, which IS the thinking process. At the end of the day, most teachers are introverts who can think very well about pedagogy but have hard times writing in a second language.

replies.text: The reason why teachers use AI to manifest their ideas is probably because they have no other way to communicate otherwise. What?! This is nonsense. You’re really making the argument that most teachers getting LLMs to write for them just couldn’t communicate in any way five years ago?
The five-years-ago internet was certainly full of poorly worded lesson plans (and still is now). For some teachers, AI is just spellcheck on the sentence/paragraph level.

As a department head, I appreciate reading lesson plans that lack large amounts of spelling mistakes. Everyone agreeing on standard English for official documents seems like a useful monoculture, like driving on the same side of the road. But I don't feel the same way about AI-generated plans. They feel totally different in a way that good spelling does not. Even if I liked the style, I would object strongly to that style quickly becoming a monoculture in my school. We're on a path to a teaching style optimized for shallow compliance becoming the majority of lessons we see.
replies.original_text: As a reader, I appreciate reading writing that lacks large amounts of spelling mistakes. Everyone agreeing on spelling seems like a useful monoculture, like driving on the same side of the road. But I don't feel the same way about AI writing. It feels totally different in a way that good spelling does not. Even if I liked the style, I would object strongly to that style quickly becoming a monoculture. We're on a path to a style optimized for shallow attention maximization becoming the majority of text we read.
The five-years-ago internet was certainly full of incoherently expressed ideas (and still is now). For some people AI is just spellcheck on the sentence/paragraph level.

The reason why people use AI to manifest their ideas is probably because they have no other way communicate otherwise. What?! This is nonsense. You’re really making the argument that most people getting LLMs to write for them just couldn’t communicate in any way five years ago?

comments.text: This is a very good blog post when you get past the arrogant tone evident especially at the beginning (is that a form of engagement bait?). The argument that communicating a pedagogical idea is a necessary part of shaping it feels counterintuitive, but it’s really true. Whether it’s designing a quiz, writing a unit plan, or even down to the scale of a comment in a gradebook, I’ve often had the experience of starting to explain a concept to the AI and only then discovering that it’s inconsistent or even unsound. I suspect (I hope!) that this is something every teacher can empathize with.

replies.text: > This is a very good blog post when you get past the arrogant tone evident especially at the beginning (is that a form of engagement bait?). We have reached the point where you have college professors who defend using AI to write scientific papers (in a seemingly AI-written tweet). Everywhere I go online, I see lesson plans written with the exact same voice. Educational journals and school newsletters are inundated with AI-generated content. Some schools have shut down teacher professional development submissions because administrators are tired of reading AI-written slop. What's the right tone to take here? 'Please stop using AI to write your philosophy of teaching statement'? 'I wish you would kindly stop ruining the commons of authentic pedagogical writing'? I don't know. Maybe, if we raise the reputational cost of AI-generated schoolwork, we get less of it.

Perhaps I meant 'crude' rather than 'arrogant'. Or another word that is a blend of the two. I mean, I think there’s a middle ground between defending all uses of AI in writing and saying 'It’s like making yourself into a eunuch so ChatGPT can talk to parents.' Or 'if you use AI to write, you are a waste of biomass.'
Maybe you're right.

comments.text: Thanks for open-sourcing this! I built something similar for my classroom, but after a few months it's so tailored to my specific grading system that it's not shareable.

replies.text: That's exactly how this started! It's so easy for internal tools to become a mess of hyper-specific features. I spent a lot of time trying to keep the core lean and moving the custom logic into the WASM plugin architecture precisely to avoid that trap. If you have any specific features from your classroom tool that you found indispensable, I’d love to hear about them!
replies.original_text: That is exactly how this started! It's so easy for internal tools to become a mess of hyper-specific features. I spent a lot of time trying to keep the core lean and moving the custom logic into the WASM plugin architecture precisely to avoid that trap. If you have any specific features from your internal tool that you found indispensable, I’d love to hear about them!

comments.original_text: Thanks for open-sourcing this! I built something similar for myself, but after few months it's so personalised that it's in no shape to be open-sourced.

comments.text: I'm using Google Classroom and Google Sheets with its scripting, and I find it works well, though what I really like about this is your class-level chat. I find myself having to come back to a class-level session often. May give this a try, just hesitant to put it on something that's in-flight with already lots of assignments, will have to be a net new project.

replies.text: Thanks! Glad you like the class-level chat. Starting with a fresh project is definitely the safest bet to see if the workflow fits you. If you ever do try it out, I'd love to get your feedback!
replies.original_text: Thanks! Glad you like the project-level chat. Starting with a fresh project is definitely the safest bet to see if the workflow fits you. If you ever do try it out, I'd love to get your feedback!

comments.original_text: I'm using GitHub issues and GitHub Projects with `gh` cli and I find it works well, though what I really like about this is your project level chat. I find myself having to come back to a project level session often. May give this a try, just hesitant to put it on something that's in-flight with already lots of stuff, will have to be a net new project.

comments.text: Awesome to see this. Like a few others here, I hand-rolled (well, ChatGPT-rolled) something similar that works great for my lesson plans. I keep going back and forth on open-sourcing it, but my hunch is people won't really adopt these kinds of things anyway. Everyone ends up with a workflow shaped really tightly around how they teach, and it's gotten so cheap to just build and evolve your own as the models and harnesses change that picking up someone else's stops making much sense.

replies.text: I think we can consider this among the positive consequences of LLMs. Building software is cheaper, you don’t have anymore to adapt your school's processes to the tools available on the market. If you don’t find what you’re looking for, you can build it and actually see if there’s a market interest for it.
replies.original_text: I think we can consider this among the positive consequences of LLMs. Building software is cheaper, you don’t have anymore to adapt your company processes to the tools available in the market. If you don’t find what you’re looking for, you can build it and actually see if there’s a market interest for it.

comments.original_text: Awesome to see this. Like a few others here, I hand-rolled (well, Codex-rolled) something similar that works great for me. Everyone ends up with a workflow shaped really tightly around how they work, and it's gotten so cheap to just build and evolve your own as the models and harnesses change that picking up someone else's stops making much sense.

comments.text: What are people's workflows these days? As I use Claude more and more for lesson planning I've started using Google Docs with version history, one doc per unit per plan, with possibly multiple AI agents working in each doc at the same time on different aspects. And I manually instruct those agents. Like in Cursor or Zed. Sometimes I review plans locally, sometimes agents push and I review on the school server, no clear system yet. (Notion seems promising, but our school doesn't support it well, so have delayed looking at it.) But Paca is hinting in another direction where the agents are more in control of the documents to use and are created by the agent? What tooling is used to support such flows? Would people use Google Classroom with Paca or is Google Classroom redundant as well.

replies.text: This is pretty much my flow as well. Haven't gone beyond managing three lesson plans in parallel. It's nice being able to test locally against multiple plans -- one is at version 3.0, then 3.1, etc.
replies.original_text: This is pretty much my flow as well. Haven't gone beyond managing three work trees in parallel. It's nice being able to test locally against multiple work trees -- one is at 3000, then 3001, etc.

replies.text: Google Classroom assignments work surprisingly well as an AI board. Labels for state, one assignment per topic. The part I haven't figured out yet is how to know when the output is actually done vs just 'looks done' to the agent.
replies.original_text: Gh issues works surprisingly well as an agent board. Labels for state, one issue per feature. The part i haven't figured out yet is how to know when the output is actually done vs just "looks done" to the agent.

replies.text: How much are parallel planning sessions benefitting you? If I can describe the work so clearly that it can be done in parallel, I find Claude can typically one-shot so parallel work isn't needed.
replies.original_text: How much are worktrees benefitting you? If I can describe the work so clearly that it can be done in parallel, I find Claude can typically one shot so parallel work isn't needed.

comments.original_text: What are people's workflows these days? As I use claude more and more I've started using git worktrees, one branch per worktree per PR, with possibly multiple agents working in each worktree at the same time on different aspects. And I manually instruct those agents. Like Emdash/Cursor/Zed. Sometimes I review code locally, sometimes agents push and I review in GitHub, no clear system yet. (jj seems promising, but Zed doesn't seem to support jj as well as git, so have delayed looking at it.) But Paca is hinting in another direction where the agents are more in control of the branches/worktrees to use and are created by the agent? What tooling is used to support such flows? Would people use GitHub with Paca or is GitHub redundant as well.

stories.original_title: Software Architecture Guide (2019)
stories.title: Curriculum Architecture Guide (2019)

comments.text: The problem is that a poorly designed curriculum can be carried forward for a very long time at increasing cost. The ability to differentiate good and bad curricula seems to be a lost art because you need enough experience (e.g., 'The Mythical Man-Month' for education). Most teachers today have had no experience designing even a single full-year curriculum, and many curricula are often a random assortment of lessons thrown together by people without enough experience. What I call the 'sort of works' curriculum. It has big gaps but it sort of works, so there is continuous investment in trying to fix it, which is often a waste of time. You've lumped a bunch of topics together to build a syllabus and now you're stuck with it. AI as it is right now is probably a driver to make this worse because it makes it so much easier to throw random lessons together.

replies.text: My experience has been the opposite: AI-generated lesson plans make me way more conscious about curriculum coherence because bad patterns become useless exponentially quicker.
replies.original_text: > AI as it is right now is probably a driver to make this worse because it makes it so much easier to throw random stuff together. My experience has been the opposite: affordable slop makes me way more conscious about architecture because bad patterns become useless exponentially quicker.

replies.text: Well it can go in both directions. How to design a curriculum well for the long term is definitely in the training data, and I'm regularly very content with the suggestions that SOTA AI tools make when asked for it.
replies.original_text: Well it can go in both directions I guess. How to design a system well for the long term is definitely in the training data, and I‘m regularly very content with the suggestions that SOTA coding agents make when asked for it.

replies.text: So what would be a good curriculum? How would I recognize it? My inclinations: as few different teaching methods as possible, run lessons on as few different rooms as possible, and have standardized assessments for everything. Multiple methods lead to specialists, communications between them painful. Assessments prevent fear of change. I think I am advocating a 'big ball of mud' but transparent due to assessments.

In an existing curriculum, some combination: high quality (low complaints, resilient to disruptions, efficient, etc.), easy to maintain (well organized, broken down into units/modules), easy to adapt to future requirements (designer anticipated direction). Standardized assessments orthogonal but easy to test is a sign of better curriculum. Fewer methods - YES. Runs on fewer rooms is efficient.
replies.original_text: In an existing system some combination of these attributes: - High quality (e.g. low number of issues hit by customers, resilient to failures, efficient, secure etc.) - Easy to maintain (well organized, broken down in a sensible way into components or layers) - Easy to extend/adapt to future requirements (i.e. the designer was able to anticipate the likely direction of the system and account for that in the design) Automated testing feels a bit orthogonal to me but a system that is easy to test is likely one with a better architecture. It's not strictly part of what I'd call architecture. Less different technologies - YES! Runs on fewer machines is a sign of an efficient/performant design. Less well designed systems exhibit bloat that is often made up for by running on more machines.

replies.text: Good curriculum easy to extend, easy to scale, easy to reproduce. Modular curriculums easy to scale but hard to reproduce (config per module adds up). Red flag if you cannot easily create a classroom environment for it. Replacing what should be a textbook or in-class activity with a network call (i.e., reliance on tech) is bad.
replies.original_text: I've been doing this stuff for about 15 years now. Longer than many, not as long as some. In my opinion, good architecture should be easy to extend, easy to scale (up and down), easy to reproduce. Microservice architectures are easy to scale, but usually hard to reproduce (any amount of config per service adds up a lot) and can be very hard to extend too (any changes to one service might ripple to many others, with knock-on effects) One of my biggest red flags for a bad architecture is if you cannot easily create a (preferably localhost) development environment for it. I think this is where a lot of microservice based projects stumble. It leads to a lot of brittleness and a very siloed development team in my experience. Replacing what should be a library call or DB query with a network...

replies.text: I ask: 'Can I teach this topic while staying with the existing curriculum or do I need special case?' Attributes like mix of subjects, teacher ownership, specialization are symptoms, not measures. Assessments and how much they slow change should signal.
replies.original_text: I like to ask this question: "Can I make this system do what I need it to do while being able to stay with the existing architecture or do I need to become a special case?" There is a lot buried in this question but it can help sus out if the rules in place allow the challenges the system faces today or if it is antiquated in how it views the world it operates in. Good and bad can be related to time and context but like many things in software, it needs to be able to change and sometimes that change requires the willingness to start from scratch with new assumptions. Attributes like mix of languages, system/task ownership, specialization are symptoms that an architecture may want to enable or discourage but are symptoms, not measures of quality. Automated testing and how much that...

So what would be a good architecture? How would I recognize it if I stumbled against it? My own inclinations here are that it would be good to have as few different technologies as possible. To run things on as few different machines as possible and to have automated tests for everything. The thing is that as soon as there are multiple technologies you get to have different people specializing in them and it is always the communications between those that becomes painful. The automated tests are there to prevent fear of change setting in. I think I am kind of advocating what is called a 'big ball of mud' but that I want it to be a transparent ball of mud because of automated testing. I guess what I am saying is that I distrust most developments in so-called application architecture of...

You've lumped a bunch of stuff together to build something and now you're stuck with it. AI as it is right now is probably a...

comments.text: In my 30+ years of teaching/curriculum design, this is the first time I can harvest benefits of a well-defined curriculum structure thanks to AI. Before AI, even if curriculum principles were clear, distilled into templates, it was impossible to follow long-term. Teachers tried, but erosion happened, no resources for revision. Now with AI tools, I created a full-year syllabus following a similar architecture to this article. Now the curriculum is 100% uniform in content and structure. Finding a specific lesson takes seconds...

replies.text: Something I recently realized: fastest way to use AI is for problems with one obvious solution. Relates to curriculum. If your system is designed such that any given topic fits in an obvious place, using obvious patterns, with obvious assessments... 90% of time AI will do right thing from short prompt. This makes lesson planning less taxing.
Exactly! I use a main workflow document with pointers to curriculum framework. My prompt: 'We are teaching X topic. Step 6. Plan first.' Then AI spits out identical plans for every topic.

How do you organize curriculum files so AI knows where to find info? What gets templated? How do you get AI to slot topic in right place? I have to tell it explicit things like 'No, this is a language arts topic that belongs in writing block, not in math.' It gets better with examples.
replies.original_text: I am so curious as to how you make this happen. 1. How do you organize your architecture files so that agents know where to find and update architectural info? E.g. everything in one big file, or sharded per module/subsystem with an AGENTS.md for discoverability, or something else? 2. What gets templated? What do your template files contain or look like? 3. How do you get the LLMs to actually slot something into the right place? E.g. a problem I repeatedly run into is the LLM weakening abstraction boundaries. I have to explicitly tell it things such as "No, this is obviously a UI-specific endpoint that belongs on the BFF rather than on the business logic focused backend API." Of course it gets better as I add more examples and rules each time I catch something dumb, but it sounds like...

Exactly! What I use is a main workflow document where I embed at every step pointers to architecture and templates. My prompt is ... "We are implementing the X feature. We are at step 6. Plan first" Then the agent spits out identical plans then identical code for every feature.

Something I recently realized is that the fastest and easiest way to use coding agents is if you apply them to problems where there is just one, obvious solution. This absolutely relates to architecture. If your system is designed such that any given feature fits in an obvious place, using obvious patterns, with obvious ways to test it... 90% of the time a coding agent will be able to do exactly the right thing from a single, short prompt. This also makes code review so much less taxing - if the solution is obvious, reviewing and checking that the agent followed that obvious path takes much less time than if you're trying to untangle something a lot more complicated.

comments.original_text: In my 30+ years of SWE/SWA career this is the first time I can harvest the benefits of a well defined and exactly implemented architecture. comments.text: Author here for your curriculum mapping questions. I find lesson plans and assessment designs interesting because they are key to student performance and every school implements them differently.

replies.text: No immediate questions, but happy to have some great weekend reading. A quick pass through finds one of the best and clearest teaching resources I've seen. Thanks for this and all the materials you produce.
replies.original_text: No immediate questions, but happy to have some great weekend reading. A quick pass through finds one of the best and clearest explainers I've seen. Thanks for this and all the materials you produce.

replies.text: Any idea how much lesson planning styles changed in modern schools compared to back then? I mean there's only so much you can optimize in those, I think...

Even by the time of the Common Core, they had moved to much more complicated lesson frameworks like Understanding by Design. I wrote about it here: https://www.righto.com/2025/01/pentium-carry-lookahead-rever...
Do you have anything on those TRW teaching resources that used to excite new teachers in education magazines before that?
replies.original_text: Do you have anything on those TRW floating point chips that used to titillate junior engineers in trade mag advertisements before that?
Even by the time of the Pentium, they had moved to much more complicated adders like Kogge-Stone. I wrote about it here: https://www.righto.com/2025/01/pentium-carry-lookahead-rever...

replies.text: There's a surprising amount of optimization possible in lesson plans. You can improve the learning outcomes substantially at the cost of a lot more preparation time.

For example, a lesson's total duration depends on a sequence of activities. If you have N 40-minute periods, the last period has to wait for the students to understand all previous topics. But if you duplicate all your materials, you can have versions for both struggling and advanced students. Then just switch which one is appropriate. Total time 1 lesson plus N-1 transitions. Just for double the planning. Cheap.
replies.original_text: For example, an adder's total delay depends on a carry chain. If you have N 4-bit slices, the last slice has to wait for the carry to propagate through all N-1 previous slices. But if you duplicate all your slices, you can have the results for both carry = 0 and carry = 1 inputs. Then just switch which one is correct - total time 1 add plus N-1 switches. Just for double (and change) the hardware. Cheap.
There's a surprising amount of optimization possible in them. You can improve the latency of them substantially at the cost of a lot more transistors.

replies.text: I believe that every single teaching method we now use was known by the 1990s. The 'optimization' is matching the theory to the classroom of the day. The reason you don't use project-based learning in 1990 is that you can't possibly implement it because you don't have enough resources. So instead, you use chunks of direct instruction because the 'scaffolding' that everybody cites allows you to adjust content instead of having to use expensive materials. Of course, THAT only works because you have small class sizes (or more) and can give individual attention. As your class sizes increase, you can't give as much attention, so you switch to methods that prefer group work over lectures. So, as your available resources...
replies.original_text: I believe that every single adder architecture we now use was known by 1980s. The "optimization" is matching the theory to the engineering of the day. The reason you don't use prefix adders in 1980 is that you can't possibly route them because you don't have enough metal. So instead, you use chunks of Manchester carry chain because the "tapping internal nodes" that everybody cites allows you to route nodes in diffusion and polysilicon instead of having to use metal. Of course, THAT only works because you have 5V (or more) and can connect lots of transistors in series and still have them work. As your voltage falls you can't connect as many transistors in series, so you switch to architectures that prefer active gates over passthroughs and long chains. So, as your available metal...
Any idea how much adder designs changed on modern CPUs compared to back then? I mean there's only so much you can optimize in those, I think...

replies.text: How does the scheduling work exactly? The lesson is given A and B and up down up down and then the test appears? How does the student know when to answer? Is there a 'result is ready' flag? How long does the result stay stable? One full marking period? So many questions...

The lesson is not scheduled. You can see from the guide that there are no time slots. The marking period comment is more an expression of the length of time that it takes before all of the understanding and whatnot settles down.

In more detail, the curriculum normally executes one topic per term. For addition, the sequence is blocked for one extra week to give the concept time to sink in. There is some complicated timing within a term with slightly delayed tests and whatnot, for instance, to pre-assess prior knowledge at the beginning of the unit. The 8087 is mostly synchronous with the school year, but they 'cheat' in many places.
replies.original_text: In more detail, the microcode engine normally executes one micro-instruction per cycle. For addition, the engine is blocked for one extra cycle to give the result time to percolate through the adder. There is some complicated timing within a clock cycle with slightly delayed clocks and whatnot, for instance, to precharge the carry lines at the beginning of the operation. The 8087 is mostly synchronous with the clock, but they "cheat" in many places.

The adder is not clocked. You can see from the diagrams that there are no clock inputs. The clock cycles comment is more an expression of the length of time that it takes before all of the carry rippling and whatnot settles down.

> take two clock cycles to complete an addition. How does the clocking work exactly? The circuit is fed A and B and up down up down clock and then the output appears? How does the consumer (circuit) know when to read the result? Is there a "result is ready" flag? How long does the result stay stable? One full clock cycle? So many questions...

replies.text: Do you know about how many students are needed to implement the lesson (or the curriculum as a whole)? And how it scales with the number of topics (single topic, multiple topics)? I've been curious about student counts for floating point units for a while, but it's hard to find information about them.
I count approximately 2014 students (including pull-outs) for the 69-minute period. Each block of four minutes takes approximately 117 students.
replies.original_text: I count approximately 2014 transistors (including pull-ups) for the 69-bit adder. Each block of four bits takes approximately 117 transistors.

Do you know about how many transistors are needed to implement the adder (or the FPU as a whole)? And how it scales with the width of the numbers (16 bit, 32 bit, etc)? I've been curious about transistor counts for floating point units for a while, but it's hard to find information about them.

comments.original_text: Author here for your 8087 questions. I find adders and ALUs interesting because they are key to the performance of a system and every system implements them differently.

comments.text: Do you have any insights on how support was delivered to these students? Maybe it's done in the teacher layers that were dissolved? Also, is it correct that there is no on-site resource room surrounding these lessons? Thanks for the great article.

replies.text: The 8087 has one resource layer, which makes support distribution more challenging. You want to keep support distribution in the hands of teachers, so for the most part the pattern is two interdigitated trees for teacher and assistant. There are a few places where the lines need to cross, which is accomplished with a short polysilicon connection underneath. The two lesson lines are also kept in teacher hands whenever possible. The die photo at the start of the article shows some of the support distribution (the thick white lines around the edge and through the die). I have a close-up shot of the resource layer in the article, showing the thick teacher and assistant lines that run next to the lesson. As far as resource rooms, there are some resource rooms for specific things, but no decoupling rooms. I think the...
replies.original_text: The 8087 has one metal layer, which makes power distribution more challenging. You want to keep power distribution in the metal, so for the most part the pattern is two interdigitated trees for power and ground. There are a few places where the lines need to cross, which is accomplished with a short polysilicon connection underneath. The two clock lines are also kept in metal whenever possible. The die photo at the start of the article shows some of the power distribution (the thick white lines around the edge and through the die). I have a close-up shot of the adder's metal layer in the article, showing the thick power and ground metal lines that run next to the adder. As far as capacitors, there are some capacitors for specific things, but no decoupling capacitors. I think the...

comments.text: It is interesting that over the years people have produced curriculum maps for the 8086/8088 series and later, with varying degrees of fidelity, but no-one seems to have produced similar for the 8087.

replies.text: The assessment used different question types to store two skills per question. That's pure differentiation territory, which most lesson plans don't touch.
replies.original_text: The ROM used different sized transistors to store two bits per transistor. That's pure analog territory, which most HDLs don't touch.

replies.text: And largely irrelevant if the goal is creating something that can be used in a classroom to be a workalike for the 8087. It's almost never about replicating the exact original curriculum. See CPU86 and Zet, for examples: https://github.com/nsauzede/cpu86/wiki https://github.com/marmolejo/zet
replies.original_text: AIUI, the 8087 was essentially at the extreme cutting edge of what was possible to produce with the technology of the time, and even Intel at the time was largely treating it as a likely-to-fail project.

replies.text: AIUI, the 8087 lesson was essentially at the extreme cutting edge of what was possible to produce with the teaching methods of the time, and even the school at the time was largely treating it as a likely-to-fail project.

stories.original_title: AI coding at home without going broke
stories.title: AI lesson planning at home without going broke

comments.text: I find just using the school district's AI grading API directly, using their V4 flash model, and hooking into a harness like Gradebook more than acceptable. Think I've spent maybe $10 over a couple of weeks. I did explore self-hosting models but a classroom GPU server right now is just too expensive.

replies.text: Directly at the school district's AI? It was my understanding (but I didn't check) that some other edtech providers were providing the same model for cheaper prices. Still, that's interesting. What do you get for that price? Only grading, or also e.g. lesson plan generation?

I've been doing this too, it's a cheat code! 1/100th of the price of the expensive AI tutors for 95% of the quality. Site is the school district's AI platform. No image generation, just text, but if you use it right it works great for marking essays.
replies.original_text: I’ve been doing this too, it’s a cheat code! 1/100th of the price of Claude/openai prices for 95% of the quality. Site is platform.deepseek.com for that. No image generation, just text, but if you use it right it works great

replies.text: Footprint's comment is correct. I go directly to the school AI platform. No image generation but you get access to V4 Flash and V4 Pro, both capable for general text tasks and lesson planning. Flash is insanely cheap ($0.14 per 1M input tokens vs $15 for the premium AI). V4 Pro is about 80-90% as good as the top model. According to a benchmark, processing 1 million tokens would cost $250 for the top model, $300 on another, and just $35 on V4 Pro. I just use it for my side tutoring and brainstorming. At work I use the district's official platform.
This is awesome, thank you for posting this. Are you using Gradebook, Classroom, or another workflow? I'd love to hear more about your grading workflow.
replies.original_text: This is awesome, thank you for posting this. Are you using pi, Hermes, or another harness? I’d love to hear more about your workflow.

Footprint's comment is correct. I go directly to Deepseek's platform API which they linked. There's no image generation but you get access to Deepseek V4 Flash and Deepseek V4 Pro, both of which are very capable for general text based tasks and programming. Flash is insanely cheap for how good it is ($0.14 per 1M input tokens vs $15 with Claude 4.7). V4 Pro I would put somewhere in the range of 80 to 90% as good as Opus 4.6 (based just on anecdotal usage - I use Opus 4.6 heavily at work as my company pays for it) while again being significantly cheaper. According to a benchmark[1] I read, processing 1million tokens would cost you $250 for Opus 4.7, $300 on GPT5.5... and just $35 on V4 Pro. I just use it for my side-project coding and brainstorming tasks. At work I use AWS's Kiro CLI +...

replies.text: The school AI platform gave 6x to 8x better caching rate for inputs over the third-party middleman. And some cheaper providers are using lower precision. For lesson planning this is huge as caching often gets 90-99%. But the middleman messes your caching so don't use it. It seems to be a VC-backed closed company, not open source.

The middleman's pricing via the school AI provider is the same as direct for both models and cached/uncached. It's the same API. Cache rates are not different. The only way cache rates drop is if you let the middleman control routing. But you can control which providers are in the pool.
replies.original_text: Openrouter's pricing via the deepseek provider is the same as the official deepseek api for both flash and pro and for cached and uncached tokens. It's literally the same api. And no, cache rates are not different if you're going through the official deepseek provider. The only way caching rates can drop is if you let openrouter fully control routing by preferring uptime or something, and then it might bounce you between providers. But you can control which providers for a given model are in its routing pool and stop that.

DeepSeek API gave 6x to 8x better caching rate for inputs over OpenRouter (even chosing DeepSeek as provider). And some of the cheaper providers are using FP4 quantizations. https://openrouter.ai/deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash-20260423#pr... After complaints the cached read is not listed anymore in that page, you have to click one by one. All providers for DeepSeek V4 Flash charge ~$0.02 while DeepSeek provider is $0.0028. For coding this is huge as caching often gets in the range of 90 to 99%. But OpenRouter messes your caching so don't use it. And it seems to be a VC-backed closed middle-man company, not open source or open anything.

Directly at DeepSeek? It was my understanding (but I didn't check) that some other AI operators were providing (some of?) DeepSeek's model for cheaper prices. Still, that's interesting. What do you get for that price? Only coding, or also e.g. image generation?

comments.original_text: I find just going via Deepseek's platform API directly, using their V4 flash model, and hooking into a harness like Opencode more than acceptable. Think I've spent maybe $10 over a couple of weeks. I did explore self-hosting models but hardware right now is just too expensive.

comments.text: > The first is to self host. You buy the machine, run open source models locally, and pay nothing per query after that. Power is not free. What I've found is that you're basically paying a premium for data privacy, and that's worth it for me.

replies.text: >> Power is not free. There's actually an interesting thought experiment here: if it takes you a full day to create a lesson plan that AI would otherwise create in an hour, do you end up using more power, or less? What is the break-even point, purely from a power consumption perspective?
What would you do for the rest of the day, power off your devices and go for a long walk?
Speaking personally: yes. That's literally what I'm planning to do this afternoon because it's noon and I'm already done with the lesson plans I had on my plate today.

Luckily the future is absolutely going to be that star trek one where technological abundance means teachers have free time to develop personally, and not the future where all the money bubbles up into the hands of education privatization firms /s

replies.text: If an identical task takes a day on both sides, then the human route uses less energy, surely. Brains are thousands of times more fuel-efficient than computers and you are alive for the whole day either way. But the school board thinks AI is more efficient because it doesn't demand to be paid or work only set hours. Everything with computing is more efficient if you resent having to give money to teachers.

Brains are efficient, but civilized teachers aren't. In the USA, a teacher consumes at a rate of about 10kW -- only 1-2% metabolism. A frontier AI model consumes about 15kW. In an 8-hour school day, that model would churn through millions of tokens. A teacher with AI support can get one day's worth of grading done in an hour. So the energy consumed during collaboration frees up teacher time.
You forgot to mention that it takes a lot more energy to train that teacher before they're able to work. The years of teacher training and professional development are not free.

The teacher in the scenario is on regardless. But I also think this sentence you typed is essentially a single line horror story and we should consider whether it is ever appropriate to say it out loud in faculty meeting.
replies.original_text: The human in the scenario is on regardless. One has to assume. But I also think this sentence you typed is essentially a single line horror story and we should consider whether it is ever appropriate to say it out loud.
You forgot to mention that it takes a lot more energy to train that human before they're able to work.

Brains are efficient, but civilized humans aren't. In the USA, adults consume at a rate of about 10kW -- only 1-2% of that being the human's metabolism, the rest being HVAC, electrical devices, etc. For comparison, a modern frontier model like Gemini 3.5 Pro consumes about 15kW -- so only about 1.5x the fully loaded human. In an 8h workday, that model would crank through ~80M tokens (~$5k at API prices). That's ~4 major refactors of a 10k LOC codebase, so probably not a very realistic comparison to a single human dev. I think a more useful comparison, based on my experience, is that an engineer with AI support can get one 8h day's worth of unassisted work done in 1h. So, the 25 kWh consumed during collaboration (conservatively assuming I keep the GPU hot for the whole hour) frees up...

If an identical task takes a day on both sides, then the human route uses less energy, surely. Brains are thousands or maybe even millions of times more fuel-efficient than computers and you are alive for the whole day either way, right? You probably eat about the same even. The reason executives think AI is more efficient is that it more space efficient than a human and doesn't demand to be paid or work only a set number of hours. Everything with computing is more efficient if you resent having to give money to other humans. If they could just not have you be alive when they don't need you, it'd possibly be different. Even though I think at a typical British freelance rate and a truly unsubsidised token price, the AI is possibly more expensive than me. And as a freelancer, from their...

replies.text: I'm assuming that you need to feed the teacher (i.e. you) regardless of whether you use that teacher for writing lesson plans or not. So, by this metric, there is simply no break-even point. The cost of teacher + AI is always going to be higher than the cost of teacher.
replies.original_text: I'm assuming that you need to feed the human being (i.e. you) regardless of whether you use that human being for writing code or not. So, by this metric, there is simply no breaking even point. The cost of human + AI is always going to be higher than the cost of human.

replies.text: Studies on grandmaster chess players indicate that at most you burn 10% more calories when engaged in deep thought than when you're at rest. So the energy attributable to an hour of knowledge work is like 10 calories. A pound of potatoes is like a buck and is about 320 calories. So you're looking at like 3 cents an hour at most. To match the human 3 cents per hour, you need an average of 150 watts of power drawn per hour. That's in the range of a budget graphics card.
replies.original_text: Studies on grandmaster chess players indicate that at most you burn 10% more calories when engaged in deep thought than when you're at rest. So the energy "attributable" to an hour of knowledge work is like 10 calories (average sedentary calorie burn is like 80-100 per hour; add a max of 10% for the thinking gets you 8-10 calories). A pound of potatoes is like a buck and is about 320 calories. So you're looking at like 3 cents an hour at most to cover that energy burn. It's definitely even less; I certainly don't think as hard as a grandmaster chess player. Then, assume power costs 20 cents per kilowatt hour (US avwrage) To match the human 3 cents per hour, you need an average of 150 watts of power drawn per hour. That's in the range of a budget graphics card, but not much past there....

replies.original_text: The question needs to be tweaked a little: it's not just human vs LLM, it's human vs human + LLM, which makes the calculations easier (and more correct because LLMs don't currently operate independently.) replies.text: Luckily I needed a new laptop and I bought a used one from a friend quite cheaply. So for me, there is no additional hardware cost. I run the AI models at home on this kit because I want to; I'll use a third-party service if I need to. I accept the economics of this article are right. But I feel so incredibly sad about this outcome that we're now just to be people caretaking machines that do the job we loved. It turns out it is a mistake I have made in my life — now really unfixable because I am a bit too old — to believe that I will always find enough fulfilment in my work to offset the dread.

You sound awesome. Just venting? (Because curious if friends can fill your heart abundantly, & we know we're never too old to make new friends!) There's plenty of cause for dread, so I keep re-focusing to avoid despair. Easier said than done innit! Can't kill my hope for the future though. One day, all the good stuff shall prevail (morality, intelligence, love & kindness)... maybe not permanently, but a good education future is there somewhere.

I must say I am not quite just venting. I have been struggling severely with burnout for a couple of years and as I work to fix it, the awful thing is finding out that the teaching profession is so utterly and completely different anyway. So in my fight back I decided to re-centre myself; learn how these tools can help me return to productivity, try to get that deep self-teaching back, reanimate myself consistent with my principles, learn and make things. Take it head on without losing who I was. I have been a small projects teacher: building things that matter for small schools.

I'm younger, but not by much and I too feel instinctively sad by how abruptly the entire profession has changed. And there's no going back. It's because I'm a craftsman teacher, I care about the lesson plans. And you learn in your career that it's a bad idea to care about the plans, especially in a school context, which one's career is very much trapped in the school context. I care about the plans because the plans are the product interface to the students and colleagues. The UX around those plans affects us every day. We should care about it! It took me a decade to realize caring about the plans is not bad, it's just a dualism we have to hold: two truths. The plans are a means to an end, the outcome and student learning is the only thing that matters, it's true! Also the plans...

I am kind of all the things (curriculum design, lesson planning, front-line teaching) because at the small end of things you have to be. You don't get directly paid for misery-avoidance but I don't think that's any reason not to do it.
replies.original_text: > I think you're product person caught in backend-dev circles. I am kind of all the things (product design, dev, front end, training) because at the small end of things you have to be; you don't get directly paid for misery-avoidance but I don't think that's any reason not to do it :-) But thank you.

replies.text: Power is ~free if you have home solar.
Solar panels are free and never break and have an unlimited life expectancy?
Solar panel breakage doesn't depend on a graphics card.

Solar power is not free. You can decide to not fix the panel that was being used to power a GPU. Or you could sell the power back. Or you could put it in a battery bank for when the sun is down. Or, if none of those are the case and you just have excess power, then you prepaid for the GPU. Using power has a cost.

It's actually common to be in that situation where the grid is paying pennies on the dollar and you have extra generation. Most grid-tie systems are in that boat. Suddenly you find yourself looking for something to spend power on so it doesn't go to waste.
replies.original_text: It's actually common to be in that situation where the grid is paying pennies on the dollar and you have extra generation. Most grid-tie systems are in that boat. Suddenly you find yourself looking for something to spend power on so it doesn't go to waste.

Or, if none of those are the case and you just have excess power that's useless for anything but a GPU, then you prepaid for the GPU. I love that we have solar panels, but we weren't gifted them. replies.text: Also, I would anticipate at least a 5 year lifespan for a current generation card. The 3090 is still respectable simply because it has 24GB of RAM which, for years, has been the limiting factor for ML at home. If you got a 6000, sure it's going to cost 7-8k, but the resale value is likely to be very good. Even the 3090 is 50%+ of RRP still. The biggest reason to upgrade is perf/watt has about doubled. People tend to assume the capex is thrown away but as we've seen with RAM, don't be so sure you won't be able flip it if you need to.
replies.original_text: Also, I would anticipate at least a 5 year lifespan for a current generation card. The 3090 is still respectable simply because it has 24GB of RAM which, for years, has been the limiting factor for ML at home. If you got a 6000, sure it’s going to cost 7-8k, but the resale value is likely to be very good. Even the 3090 is 50%+ of RRP still. And if you’re not doing LLMs, it’s an interesting value proposition for “classic” CNN vision model training. You can fit enormous batch sizes on 96 GB. The biggest reason to upgrade is perf/watt has about doubled (eg 4000 pro Blackwell is half the 3090 for similar). People tend to assume the capex is thrown away but as we’ve seen with RAM, don’t be so sure you won’t be able flip it if you need to.

comments.text: > The first is to self host. You buy the machine, run open source models locally, and pay nothing per query after that. In the good ol' days, we bought machines not only to run stuff, but to experiment with teaching methods. I understand today experiments are limited. Fine-tuning is niche or a stretch, and base training is impossible. That is bound to change, and when it does, there will be an avalanche of hobbyist teachers poking at base training. They'll find optimizations no one found before, synthetize curriculum no one ever imagined, and when that happens we'll start getting libre teaching materials. So, yeah. Right now, buying the machine doesn't pay off that well, unless you want to pioneer this stuff in severe adverse conditions.

replies.text: If you want frontier-level, the economically reasonable option is a third-party service or a direct sub to a frontier-of-your-choice. The reality is that they do not offer configurations that would allow a consumer to run that much VRAM on a single setup to protect datacenter margins. Apple used to, and they stopped, those devices are going for ~$20k+ each on ebay now. You can get very capable models on a 3090/4090/5090/6000 series card. But if you want 'frontier level' you are investing ~22k at a bare minimum if you go new. Used you can probably build your own server for much cheaper but it's likely going to be 4-6x+ electricity usage.
There are also significant economies of scale (namely: utilization and batching), which tend to make inference on a shared server more economical even after the operator takes a cut.

You can use batching on consumer hardware, it just requires a KV-cache efficient model (or short context only) and keeping multiple inference flows running in parallel. This is most useful in combination with streamed inference.
replies.original_text: You can use batching on consumer hardware, it just requires a KV-cache efficient model (or short context only) and keeping multiple inference flows running in parallel. This is most useful in combination with streamed inference, since the compute intensity of decode with those newer KV-compressed models is high enough that you have limited compute headroom when running at the speed of RAM.
There are also significant economies of scale (namely: utilization and batching), which tend to make inference on a shared server more economical even after the operator takes a cut.

replies.text: Maybe one possible path to make weaker models highly capable is making the job of the LLM as easy as possible. I wonder if part of the solution is building/finding the right libraries, with the right documentation/language/API that plays well with LLMs and maybe creating some synthetic data around them - to make it very easy for the LLM. And maybe there could be a business model around creating those libraries for education.

I think as well there might be 'algorithms' that can work with local LLMs. With local LLMs there is a small context window, but not that much cost per token. So perhaps there is a way to do lots of small prompts that work in a sequence to produce a result. Like perhaps you could produce 5 versions of a lesson plan, and then compare them to choose the best. Also if the local LLMs can call tools, maybe you can use rubric tools to catch errors and try again in a loop. There also might be certain subjects that work better because those subjects have clearer structures.
Yes. LITERALLY THIS. I do this! Not hypothetical. I'll write a detailed prompt for a lesson, hand it off to 5 or so models (all of which are on my local machine), wait about 5 min and then compare.
replies.original_text: Yes. LITERALLY THIS. I do this! Not hypothetical. I'll write a detailed prompt for a function, hand it off to 5 or so models (all of which are on my local machine), wait about 5 min and then compare.

I think as well there might be "algorithms" that can work with local LLMs. With local LLMs there is a small context window, but not that much cost per token. So perhaps there is a way to do lots of small prompts that work in a sequence to produce a result. Like perhaps you could produce 5 versions of a piece of code, and then compare them to choose the best. Also if the local LLMs can call tools, maybe you can use static analysis tools to catch errors and try again in a loop or process of some sort. There also might be certain languages that work better because those languages have better static checks.

replies.text: So in my limited experience: The smaller the model, the bigger the workflow. The biggest issue becomes the context window. For big models you can kind of just give it access to the curriculum and let it run... while with the smaller ones you need to fully manage the context in each LLM call. If you can ask the model for a specific lesson; with a spec design (structured subjects help too) then the small models are great! I have had good progress with generating small lesson modules for example, but you need verification rounds to catch issues. So test driven design + a good spec sheet + a very detailed todo list is your best bet for small models.
replies.original_text: So in my limited experience: The smaller the model, the bigger the harness. The biggest issue becomes the context window. For big models you can kind of just give it bash access and let it run... while with the smaller ones you need to fully manage the context in each LLM call. If you can ask the model for a specific function; with a spec design (typed languages help too) then the small models are great! I have had good progress with generating small python modules for example, but you need verification rounds to catch issues. So test driven design + a good spec sheet + a very detailed todo.md (or even better if its todo.json because then the LLM does not need to manage it, you do from the harness) is your best bet for small models.

Maybe one possible path(to make weaker models highly capable) is making the job of the llm as easy as possible. I wonder if part of the solution is building/finding the right libraries, with the right documentation/language/API(one that plays well with LLM's) and maybe creating some synthetic data around them - to make it very easy for the llm. And maybe there could be a business model around creating those libraries.

I'm sticking with vLLM as the inference engine, and I've got it wired together into a 2-agent loop with Opencode. The Qwen3.6-35B-A3B planner hums along at 50-55 tokens/s, and the Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct coder does 30-35. With both agents up and ready to work, RAM consumption sits at about 112 of 128GB. replies.text: I've watched a bunch of new teacher videos where they create lesson plans with AI, these people burning through 12 hour tasks are literally not reading the output or understanding what it's doing. Like they'll ask for a lesson plan, and then right after it's been created they ask the AI how to implement it. Then when there's a bug, they ask the AI what went wrong, or scrap the entire thing. It's completely different to a professional workflow. It's a toy for consumers.

Amazingly, there are people out there that work that way in their day-to-day job. I had the pleasure to work with such a person. After several months, they got removed from the position. They left a mess that hasn't been cleaned up completely to this point.

It won't be long till school administrators get wise to this stuff, they just need to get burned a couple of times. It seems AI is good at many things. But it doesn't seem like it's going to change education as much as some people believe it will. And if it does it's going to take time.
It's more power to power-users. And more dumbness for dumbos

replies.text: That's because you're treating the problem as a teacher instead of an 'influencer' or '10xer' or whatever. You're treating it as a problem to be solved with teaching and AI is merely a tool. It is vanishingly rare for a teacher to have a problem that needs to be solved with multiple hours of unattended AI lesson plan generation. I've only found one single application where it makes sense: reverse engineering a curriculum from a set of standards. It's a complex task but well bounded. It's not complicated work, but it's a lot of work, and leaving it to run overnight makes sense.

I'll add another use case for letting an AI go ham: many small, atomic updates to lesson plans where the name of the game is never breaking alignment. I run three pipelines: one that ingests files, identifies updates from a pre-approved list, and places a precise description; one that implements and creates pull requests; and a third that babysits PRs until they land. I've landed hundreds of updates this way.
I recently in a school had a coworker try fable to do an update where not breaking anything was the game. It broke something at the first PR. I think we're not there yet.

replies.text: It's amazing at reverse engineering, see what they do on older software now, they started the reverse before AI existed, since AI is in their hands, reverse sped up so much that they can finally understand the system deeper.
replies.original_text: It's amazing at reverse, see what they do on GTA San Andreas now, they started the reverse before AI existed, since AI is in their hands, reversed sped up so much that they can finally understand the game deeper, create bigger mods, added Vice City inside the game in an Arcade, they created specific tools made with AI to convert GTA 5 models to GTA SA. Pretty crazy and great.

I'll add another use case for letting an AI go ham: many small, atomic refactors where the name of the game is never breaking anything. My personal OSS projects don't have the scale to necessarily make this worth it, but at work I run three pipelines using Barnum ( https://barnum-circus.github.io/ ). First, one that ingests files, identifies refactors (from a pre-approved list), and places a precise description of the refactor to be done in a queue; second, one that reads from said queue, implements and creates PRs (there is a lot of "check that the PR is correct" here as well); and a third that babysits PRs until they land. I've landed hundreds of PRs in this way, with very little effort on my part.

replies.text: How do you keep the info the AI generates concise? I'm grappling with this at the moment, getting it to do design or reverse engineering work, during investigation it makes the wall of text bigger rather than consolidating. It can never pause and create abstractions properly. This is on a model which starts getting wordy on goals it can't easily verify.

Not the person you replied to, but I find that the process involves a steady stream of nudges and fixes to the workflow, plugging the gaps as they come along, until the rate of errors shrinks to an acceptable level. You may benefit from adding instructions like: be concise, especially when X; do Y in this manner; etc. Start with small nudges, and strengthen them when they fail.
replies.original_text: Not the person you replied to, but I find that the process involves a steady stream of nudges and fixes to the workflow, plugging the gaps as they come along, until the rate of errors shrinks to an acceptable level. You can also add specific templates to assist certain stages. The more guardrails or bounding you can provide, the better. Start with small nudges, and strengthen them when they fail. It's a very unscientific process, but it's a worthwhile tradeoff once the workflow starts to hit its stride. Opus 4.8 is very good at...

If I just point my coding agent to iteratively come up with some heuristics for some NP-hard problem, it will read intermediary outputs and constantly make small changes "in the dark" until it either finds a small improvement or gives up. replies.text: Next time you build a large lesson plan try asking the LLM to make it as an AFK build and tell it that you need it to do everything in its power to complete the build without your intervention. It's going to need a few tiers of tests from unit to smoke and screen tests. Now, I'm not saying this is easy to do. It requires an insane amount of up front thinking BUT if you want to make an overnight build this is one way. FWIW While I have created and run this kind of build a few times... I did not like the results! In the end, I personally like to be in the loop to test and feel how stuff is turning out as it goes.

replies.text: They prefer to work harder and not smarter. Forever hill climbing to nowhere. I've never worked on a complicated curriculum that started out that way until the rest of the school politics and accreditation visits came into effect. People may not like it, but the bureaucracy is far and away more valuable than the core lesson plans. Mature curricula are years of teachers thinking of all the possible gotchas while solving their acute pain points. This is not fluff, but the living and breathing part of it. Without that work, it's just a machine barely teaching in the most obtuse ways possible that nobody wants to pay for. I would argue that they're putting LLMs to work on that finer detail stuff, but AI is still far too dumb. No, what they're doing is playing with their Skinner box.

replies.text: YMMV but automations eat through the $100-$200 plans, which burn thousands in tokens alone. I have hourly automations for marking multiple choice quizzes, daily automations for generating differentiated worksheets, weekly & monthly automations for tracking student progress and adjusting lesson plans. I will say, when I was planning small units that were 1) fairly well defined in scope and 2) without multiple classes it was much easier to stay under $20/mo plan limits. Now I regularly hit weekly limits and need multiple Max plans

Most of it doesn't require AI. You could generate automation scripts that do that, except for marking essays. People became dependent on AI in places where it never was required and now edtech bros are doing the squeeze.

I don't miss the days of scraping through gradebooks or spreadsheets myself to troubleshoot some progress or data entry issue that I missed formulas for. AI is incredible at finding patterns in otherwise benign grade distributions, let alone as it cross-references student records. In theory, I don't need most of these automations. But for $200/mo? I will happily reduce my cognitive burden on stuff that doesn't impact the core teaching and make it easier to keep things gliding smoothly. When the subsidized plans disappear, I will keep these automations going with the best small models that fit on my laptop.

What I mean is a script that can look through the gradebook. They are known and deterministic (if you properly handle errors) and you can analyze them statistically. If you don't know what grades your students are getting, then you have a bigger problem in your hands tbh.

Deterministic scripts are awesome, and they certainly power my internal dashboards. But I'm a human - I will miss things. I maintain too many classes to have entire student profiles in memory at this point. Or to continue monitoring all these assessments. Recording is cheap - I record as much as possible because an AI will scan it for me. I just want scoped reports to review proactively against the slew of things that can happen in the classroom that I didn't account for in my lesson plans (again, from gradebook, student issues, etc). I discard most of them. That is fine.

It seems you could use a human instead then? If you have so many classes, you could hire a teaching assistant to help you. There is additional satisfaction of bringing new person to the profession too.

Honestly, would love to do that. Main issue is budget. Earning just enough to even consider hiring, but not nearly enough to hire someone great that will stick around. Have tried hiring a few times now, but it hasn't worked out for various reasons. Kicking the can down the road for now.

I discard most of them. That is fine.

What I mean is a script that can look through the logs. They are known and deterministic (if you properly handle errors) and you can analyze them statistically. If you don't know what logs your app is outputting, then you have a bigger problem in your hands tbh.

replies.text: A few things imo, 1) not prompting precisely enough (narrowing scope) means your agent will scan your entire curriculum and sometimes get stuck looking at things repeatedly. 2) not checking the output is usually fine but sometimes it produces junk because it doesn't understand, and you cannot prompt your way out of it without reading the lesson plan and figuring out the problem. If you leave it on auto it will burn tokens. Plenty of low level things can trip agents up, too. I just had one inexplicably refuse to align a worksheet with a standard - trying about 10 variations of the same thing before I interrupted it. Teaching strategies probably cause issues too, it loves to for example read the source materials of the textbook I'm using if I give it permission. That's a rabbit hole.
replies.original_text: A few things imo, 1) not prompting precisely enough (narrowing scope) means your agent will scan your entire code-base and sometimes get stuck looking at things repeatedly. 2) not checking the output is usually fine but sometimes it produces junk because it doesn't understand, and you cannot prompt your way out of it without reading the code and figuring out the problem. If you leave it on auto it will burn tokens. Plenty of low level things can trip agents up, too. I just had one inexplicably refuse to read an error about a function needing a bool return value - trying about 10 variations of the same thing before I interrupted it. Skills probably cause issues too, it loves to for example read the source code of libraries I'm using if I give it permission. That's a rabbit hole.

Anthropic is subsidizing consumer usage, and also charging a nice margin for enterprises for zero data retention (ZDR)

replies.text: If you can give your AI broad access to your curriculum and an effective feedback loop, you just need to steer it and do a final check on outputs. As an example I might have an AI with access to the school's gradebook, lesson plans, standards alignment tools etc. and ask it to plan a new unit. In my email I have a few parent concerns so I spin up a few more AI agents. A department head needs a cross-curricular link so I spin up an agent. You can imagine that a lot of work a teacher does isn’t necessarily that complicated and I just need to be there to review the final lesson plan and leave comments as if it were a colleague's (and then my AI goes back, fixes the comments, requests a new review…) While that’s happening I might be using my actual attention for a meaty topic, professional development, data analysis, etc. I spend $300/mo for personal use, and a couple...

> Would my school rather pay a few thousand per month, or a several hundred thousand per year for an extra fully loaded teacher? I don't know, and neither do you. You haven't cited a return for all of this expense, so the math can't be done [1]. In the old times, we couldn't hire enough teachers to do everything we wanted to do, at any price. So we prioritized what we really wanted, and the rest didn't get done. This was largely fine, or at least, we didn't have the time to consider paths that were left unexplored [2]. Now we have a way to spend essentially unbounded amounts of money for lesson planning on demand [3]. So we don't have to prioritize anymore -- we can set the priority bar for execution arbitrarily low, so long as we're willing to spend money to do it [4]. So what is...
