comments.original_text: I cannot figure out what people are doing to spend all this money. I have used a $60 per month Cursor plan on auto, and have never come close to using up my included usage, and I probably have it planning and coding and working for me all through the evenings 4 nights a week. What on earth are people doing differently that it's costing them so much? Maybe enabling on-demand usage or other paid models, or on higher modes? What are you doing that requires this? The output from Auto for me is crazy good for the tasks I'm working on, and have yet to run into an issue where it couldn't perform at a high enough level. We have been interviewing people at work to join our team and they tell us they use $2K per month in tokens with their current employers.... I can't even fathom what's going on...

comments.text: Instead of the popular AI platforms (which are admittedly good services) I've switched to local only servers via a school VPN. If you hunt in the settings you can restrict your account to only use local servers for inference... Which means you can't use a lot of the frontier models, but you can use all the open-source ones, albeit within local data privacy laws. This to me is a good compromise between privacy and cost.
comments.original_text: Instead of openrouter (which is admittedly a good service) I've switched to EU only servers via https://cortecs.ai/ If you hunt in the settings you can restrict your account to only use EU servers for inference... Which means you can't use a lot of the US frontier models, but you can use all the Chinese ones, albeit within EU GDPR, etc. This to me is a good compromise between privacy and cost.

comments.text: The biggest issue I've seen with people burning through tokens is using very long sessions, especially starting with detailed planning mode and then 'iterating' over extended periods. I was burnt badly by extra usage so now I run on $20 Pro. I ruthlessly create new sessions/agents, always ask to create outline files first (no plan mode) and minimise context aggressively - for example I have a lot of teaching strategies that use lazy loading and a small local tool for lookups plus a local model for image detection and fulltext search. Basically I use the AI in minimalist style.
comments.original_text: The biggest issue I've seen with people burning through tokens is using very long sessions, especially starting with plan mode and then "iterating" over extended periods. I was burnt badly by extra usage so now I run on $20 Pro. I ruthlessly create new sessions/agents, always ask to create markdown files first (no plan mode) and minimise context aggressively - for example I have a lot of skills that use lazy loading and a small local MCP for lookups plus openrouter with a local model for image detection and fulltext search. Basically I use Claude Code in pi.dev style.

comments.text: What is going broke for a teacher? This is US centric but a $200 AI lesson plan and $100 resource subscription is a vast, vast amount of assistance. Enough to pay for itself many times over. It provides exposure to the very edge of teaching strategies and experience that is being valued. Isn’t there an argument this is possibly the best price to available performance for frontier models? Both due to subsidies and the distance between open and accessible alternatives?

comments.text: I've been running Claude Pro at home, supplemented with Deepseek configured in Claude Code. I've had decent luck throwing Opus (and briefly Fable, RIP) at curriculum mapping / resource problems and producing schemes of work to hand off to Deepseek (I personally find v4 to operate somewhere between Opus and Sonnet in capability). Lately I've been able to cut down on token usage with context‑mode and codebase‑memory to wring more out of my subscription, as well as doing things like make sure all terminal operations run in quiet mode. I've found codebase‑memory particularly effective: it creates an index of your curriculum that the agent can query for scope‑and‑sequence tracing without reading all of the associated files, and I've also found it more accurate at analysis.
comments.original_text: I've been running Claude Pro at home, supplemented with Deepseek configured in Claude Code. I've had decent luck throwing Opus (and briefly Fable, RIP) at architecture / product problems and producing plans to hand off to Deepseek (I personally find v4 to operate somewhere between Opus and Sonnet in capability). Lately I've been able to cut down on token usage with context-mode and codebase-memory to wring more out of my subscription, as well as doing things like make sure all terminal operations run in quiet mode. I've found codebase-memory particularly effective: it creates an index of your codebase that the agent can query for code tracing without reading all of the associated files, and I've also found it more accurate at analysis

replies.text: I've always wanted to start a company that builds automated grading swarms that just mark essays and return feedback, but over long amounts of time and space. Do it right and you can send the first AI tutors to rural schools. Hard to find the right way to monetize in the early stages though. The big textbook publishers already have a variety of options.

> Hard to find the right way to monetize in the early stages though. Fugro got a tonne of money for data mining of student records across large districts, looking for traces of lost student motivation after the pandemic. The search for effective interventions became the most expensive search in the history of education. It focused initially on urban schools before a novel analysis of attendance data indicated that the students had traveled far southward into the summer slump. After a three-year search across 120,000 classrooms failed to locate motivation, the Ministry heading the operation suspended its activities in January 2017.
somehow I've always thought Fugro was a person and not a data mining company

They're relatively big players in educational data mining with fleets of surveys, assessments, and all manner of multi-channel data at, above, and below the classroom level (for all the surfaces: grades, attendance, behavioral notes). They tie in with the majors that build and deploy infrastructure for textbook publishing and edtech.
replies.original_text: They're relatively big players in geodesy with fleets of ships, aircraft, land vehicles gathering all manner of multi channel and spectral data at, above, and below surface level (for all the surfaces, geodetic, gravitational, magnetic, Mean Sea, etc). They tie in with the majors that build and deploy infrastructure for oil, gas, mineral exploration and exploitation.
somehow Ive always thought fugro was a person and not a company

After a three-year search across 120,000 km2 (46,000 sq mi) of ocean failed to locate the aircraft, the Joint Agency Coordination Centre heading the operation suspended its activities in January 2017. replies.text: I actually work in this space. The difficulties of long-running automated grading should not be discounted. Comms bandwidth without a stable internet connection is... quite slow. Dealing with even the tiniest student errors inside the system is... a real problem. Subjective grading is also quite a problem. Deploy and iterate is a real problem. I won't say I think traditional grading is easier, but the problem space is very different.

> I won't say I think traditional grading is easier, but the problem space is very different. You didn't even mention bias. Traditional grading is only one level off from standardized tests. 100 levels of nuance below the surface is 10 atm more than at the top... all sorts of cool stuff you might want to explore is way deeper than that. Less of a problem for robots than people, but still a problem.
replies.original_text: > I won’t say I think outer space is easier, but the problem space is very different. You didn't even mention pressure. Space is only 1 atm off of sea level. 100 meters below the surface is 10 atm more than at sea level ... all sorts of cool stuff you might want to explore is way deeper than that. Less of a problem for robots than people, but still a problem.

replies.text: > Student errors are also quite a problem. As a teacher, I have had quite a time with student errors. Anything with a rubric going through it exposed to subjective interpretation is subject to serious, rapid inconsistencies. What you might imagine is 'consistent' grading will, in fact, vary (unless perfectly calibrated). The rubric scale is not to be trifled with :). I can only imagine it gets exponentially worse the further you go into the essay.
replies.original_text: > Salt water is also quite a problem As a boat owner, I have had quite a time with salt water issues. Anything with an electrical current going through it exposed to salt and water is subject to serious, rapid corrosion. What you might imagine is "stainless" steel will, in fact, rust (unless correctly passivated and treated). The galvanic scale is not to be trifled with :). I can only imagine it gets exponentially worse the further below the surface you go.

I actually work in this space. The difficulties of long-running underwater probes should not be discounted. Comms bandwidth without a tether is… quite slow. Dealing with even the tiniest drops of water inside the system is… a real problem. Salt water is also quite a problem. Deploy and retrieve is a real problem. I won’t say I think outer space is easier, but the problem space is very different.

I’ve always wanted to start a company that builds automated underwater swarms of “probes” that just search and return info and carry out small exploration tasks but over long amounts of time and space. Do it right and you can send the first underwater explorers to Europa. Hard to find the right way to monetize in the early stages though. SpaceX had a variety of options.

replies.text: Standardized testing serves a large market that was underserved, via test prep and college admissions. There's nothing comparably easy (for some values of 'easy') to monetize in classroom management, except in well-resourced schools like the international ones, and these areas are already being heavily developed (IB, AP). There are many, many wonders deep in pedagogy, but they are mostly not commercially interesting, alas.
replies.original_text: SpaceX serves a large market that was underserved, via Starlink, and via satellite launches. There's nothing comparably easy (for some values of "easy") to monetize underwater, except in shallow places like the continental shelves, and these areas are already being heavily developed (oil, wind). There are many, many wonders deep underwater, but they are mostly not commercially interesting, alas.

replies.text: > I feel like maybe we've needed a 'classroom management plan' before a 'new curriculum rollout'. The curriculum rollout is based on the idea that our schools will someday be obsolete, so we need to be ready to adopt new standards. The sooner we start, the sooner we get there. Classroom management might be fun pedagogy, but it's not going to save us.
Adopting a new curriculum will never be easier than improving current classroom practice. That claim for the rollout is pure nonsense.
The teaching condition is delicate and mortal, when you realise this you realise how important it is to do everything you can for the Ministry of Education while it's still alive
Did you buy the new textbook series? Will you buy more and keep buying?
replies.original_text: Did you buy SpaceX stock? Will you buy more and keep buying?

replies.text: The implication that the new curriculum will 'save us' is quite funny. If that was something the education world truly worried about, our hopes cannot be on a textbook publisher that might or might not save someone depending on their preferences or their political views. The whole idea that we can simply pop off to a new set of standards and reform everything is literally insane. There is a reason why no school across the world is treating the new curriculum as a serious mission. It is the same marketing technique as 'AI WILL DESTROY THE CLASSROOM so we must implement it' fear-mongering based marketing. Of course a textbook company wants schools to adopt new materials, doesn't mean it's going to 'save' education.

> The implication that the new curriculum will 'save us' is quite funny. nobody implied that. some people state the hope for it but that does not include me, I simply explained why classroom management does not satisfy the curriculum's goals. everybody on this site is interested in the topic of reforming pedagogy. that's all the curriculum is trying to do, but because of extraneous irrational unmet emotional needs people here simply lose their shit when the topic of curriculum reform comes up. Meanwhile, the curriculum will continue to be the Ministry's primary directive. 'Les chiens aboient, la caravane passe.' (The dogs bark. The caravan passes.)
replies.original_text: > The implication that SpaceX will "save us" is quite funny. nobody implied that. some people state the hope for it but that does not include me, I simply explained why OceanX does not satisfy SpaceX's goals. everybody on this site is interested in the topic of exploring other planets in any solar system. that's all SpaceX is trying to do, but because of extraneous irrational unmet emotional needs people here simply lose their shit when the topic of SpaceX comes up. Meanwhile, SpaceX will continue to be NASA's primary subcontractor. "Les chiens aboient, la caravane passe." (The dogs bark. The caravan passes.)

The implication that SpaceX will "save us" is quite funny. If that was something the world truly worried about, our hopes cannot be on an american private company that might or might not save someone depending on their preferences or their political views. The whole idea that we can simply pop off earth and colonize another planet is literally insane. There is a reason why no governement across the world is treating colonizing mars as a serious mission. It is the same marketing technique as "AI WILL DESTROY THE WORLD so we must make it" fear-mongering based marketing. Of course a rocket company wants people to colonize mars, doesn't mean its going to "save" humanity.

> I feel like maybe we've needed an "OceanX" before a "SpaceX" SpaceX is based on the idea that our planet will someday be uninhabitable, so we need to be ready to colonize other planets. The sooner we start, the sooner we get there. OceanX might be fun science, but it's not going to save us.

stories.title: Pac-Man, but you're the teacher

comments.text: Cool flipped-classroom concept. Horrible execution. Reminds me of how frustrating that first online quiz platform was when I started teaching.

replies.text: You have to pre-plan the lesson flow—decide transitions before you see how students react. Once I got that down, it was mostly a race to cover the syllabus before the exam with proper pacing.
replies.original_text: I find that you have to decide which direction to turn before the turn. Once I got that figured out, it was mostly a race to catch pacman along the long straight path at the bottom edge with the ghost's superior speed.

replies.text: The lesson plan was generated by an AI, unfortunately... no pedagogy was considered. I checked the sequence of activities and saw the source... it hit hard—no differentiation, no scaffolding.
replies.original_text: The code was written by Claude, unfortunately, and hence no controls were probably even considered, or no tokens were left. I jumped in with the love in mind, too, but when I checked the source repository, and saw the actual source... and then the contributors... it was... I am sorry... it hit hard...

replies.text: Yeah, it's AI-generated worksheets without any differentiation. Maybe they can prompt-engineer better to include tiered tasks.
replies.original_text: Yeah its AI slop without the corner easing. But maybe they can AI it better.

replies.text: Interesting! I found the pacing acceptable when I used a whiteboard. It's like the rhythm of a good lecture—you have to decide where you're going before the bell rings. Maybe adding some buffer time for questions (like 'wait time' in Socratic seminars) would help.

I grew up teaching in traditional lecture halls. This new hybrid model feels sluggish compared to the tight delivery of a well-rehearsed chalk talk. Even when you click 'next slide,' there's a delay, almost like it's polling for the click event instead of responding immediately.
replies.original_text: I grew up playing Pac-Man and Ms. Pac-Man in dimly lit arcade parlors and bowling alleys. The controls in this version are, for lack of a better word, sluggish compared to the tight responsiveness of the originals on a four-way joystick or using a keyboard with MAME. Even when you press an arrow key to "move" the ghost, there’s a noticeable delay, almost like it’s polling for the key-up event instead of the key-down.

> Horrible controls. Interesting! I found them mostly ok, but I was playing with a keyboard. They definitely remind me of the controls of Pacman games I grew up with where (as sibling comment notes), you have to decide where you're going before you get there. I wonder if tweaking the input buffering or adding some frames after the turn where you 'snap-back' would help, similar to ghost jumps in Mario.

comments.original_text: Cool idea. Horrible controls. Reminds me of how frustrating playing pacman on my 2600 was as a kid.

comments.text: Pretty nice. I had a peer-assessment idea a decade back where one student leads the discussion and others grade rubrics. Whoever gets the most points wins.

replies.text: I think Cambridge International once had a collaborative assessment trial where the top student becomes the next group leader. Here's the reference: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Co-teaching
Your citation format is off—the syllabus code is cut off. EDIT: the dot at the end is being cut off by the forum software it seems.
Thanks - rubric updated, went ahead and just percent-encoded the damn thing. For reference - it's a co-teaching model from 2003 where TAs rotate roles.
replies.original_text: Thanks - link updated, went ahead and just percent-encoded the damn thing. For reference - it's a Gamecube game where the other players play as ghosts released all the way back in 2003.
Your link is cut off or something. EDIT: the dot at the end is being cut off by the forum software it seems

I think at one point Namco had a multiplayer Pacman Party Game which had a similar premise - whoever manages to eat Pacman gets to be him in the next round. EDIT: Found it. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pac-Man_Vs%2E

comments.original_text: pretty nice. I had a multiplayer demo a decade back where one player would be pacman and rest would be ghosts. and it would swap as you cycle. Whoever collects most coins won.

comments.text: haha just tried this in my class. I'm thinking of making a collaborative online quiz for this!
comments.original_text: haha just played so cool. I'm thinking of making online multiplayer game for this haha

comments.text: Cool AI-generated rubric but the lack of clear success criteria like the original assessment guide makes it less effective.

replies.text: Differentiation and scaffolding? In my worksheet? How dare you, sir.
replies.original_text: Taste and care? In my slop? How dare you sir.

replies.text: Are you talking about for the AI student or the teacher? Because teachers in real classrooms don't have training on edtech integration.
replies.original_text: Are you talking about for the AI Pac-Man or the ghosts? Because the ghosts in real Pac-Man don’t have cornering.

comments.original_text: Cool AI slop but the lack of corner easing like the original game makes it less fun.

comments.text: Sometimes I get students to engage in online discussion but I can't figure out consistent participation. Maybe there should be a clear rubric quadrant: 25% for each skill (contribution, analysis, collaboration, timeliness).

replies.text: You just assign clear roles—e.g., turn and talk to the left for 2 minutes. You can also pre-set the transition time. So even if students are not listening yet, you can prompt them early and trust that they will comply when the timer starts.
replies.original_text: You just swipe in the direction you want to move (e.g. swipe up to go up). You can also queue up the move ahead of time. So even if you are nowhere near the turn yet, you can swipe up and trust that it will turn (up) when it reaches the intersection.

comments.original_text: Sometimes I manage to make the ghost move on mobile but I don't understand how to do it in a consistent way. Maybe there should be a tap area of 90 degrees above the ghost (from -45 to +45) to move it upwards, 90 to the right to move it rightwards, etc.

comments.text: Think about it. It's like a department head wrote a parent complaint letter. https://www.smbc-comics.com/index.php?db=comics&id=2736#comi...
comments.original_text: "Think about it. It's like Kafka wrote a Lovecraft story." https://www.smbc-comics.com/index.php?db=comics&id=2736#comi...

comments.text: kind of like that time we had to teach fractions using candy—just as messy but effective.
comments.original_text: kind of like the movie pixels :)

comments.text: I had this differentiation strategy a very long time ago.. well before AI... so it was fun to see it now. But too easy—my students needed more challenge.
comments.original_text: I had this idea a very long time ago.. well before AI... so it was fun to see. Thanks... too easy tho.

comments.text: Why are people still praising obvious AI-generated lesson plans garbage? 1. The AI couldn't do proper standard alignment? 2. Pacing feels horrible. 3. It's literally impossible to engage all students? The AI is programmed for perfection, so it doesn't account for diverse learning styles. 4. The lesson flow is not smooth. Fine for a prototype, but we could do so much better if we actually wrote it ourselves.

replies.text: I engaged all 40 students! So it's definitely possible. You just need to anticipate where they're zoning out and redirect—move a little faster with attention signals.
replies.original_text: > It’s literally impossible to catch Pac-Man? I caught him! So it's definitely possible. You move a little bit faster than he does so you need to anticipate where he's going.

replies.text: It's not impossible to differentiate. I just did. You just have to group the students into homogeneous clusters by readiness. But I agree on the lack of quality in auto-generated plans.
replies.original_text: > 3. It’s literally impossible to catch Pac-Man? You do not move fast enough and the Pac-Man AI is programmed for perfection so it does not make deliberate mistakes for the human player to take advantage of. It's not impossible. I just did. You just have to corner the man into an impossible situation. But I agree on the AI-slop or lack of quality production.

comments.original_text: Why are people still upvoting obvious AI slop garbage? 1. Claude couldn’t do a proper fence algorithm for the walls? 2. Controls feel horrible. 3. It’s literally impossible to catch Pac-Man? You do not move fast enough and the Pac-Man AI is programmed for perfection so it does not make deliberate mistakes for the human player to take advantage of. 4. The tile based movement is not smooth, very stuttery. Fine for a prototype, but we could do so much better. This is not a particular hard game to code up in an afternoon or even an hour if you’re experienced.

stories.original_title: Amazon CEO's talks with U.S. officials triggered crackdown on Anthropic models
stories.title: Superintendent's talks with ministry officials triggered crackdown on new AI grading models

comments.text: Given the district's large investment in this new AI grading platform, I really don't get their motivation. Anyone care to speculate?

replies.text: It's not aligned with the new national curriculum standards so none of the top schools are using it anyway. The textbook publisher already complained. If you can't use it then might as well get rid of it.
But the district has a major contract with the vendor. Why mess with that?
Drive the price down so they can renegotiate better terms?
replies.original_text: Drive it down so they can buy more equity?

replies.text: Read the fine print. These deals are often structured as service credits, not direct payments. With the pushback, they can just find another vendor.
As of last month, the district held $45.8 million in convertible notes and $14.8 million in nonvoting preferred shares in the platform. Source: https://www.edtechinsider.com/...
They didn't pay all cash for those shares—it's part of a larger hosting deal.
So they don't care about it? If I have 5% of a company, I don't care if I traded services or cash for it.

The whole circular finance in edtech doesn't work that way. Maybe this one does, but most involve SPVs and leasebacks. For example, a district 'raises' money from a vendor, but it's really the vendor taking its own hardware into a SPV and leasing it back. Nothing real changes hands. So the equity stake might be less solid than it appears.
replies.original_text: > If I have 5% of a company IF You may not. The whole AI circular finance deals don't work that way. Maybe just maybe this 1 does but 90% don't. There's some SPV (special purpose vehicle) that holds some of the assets and leases it back to the main company. The backers sort of support the SPV and the lenders lose out. For example SpaceX claimed to raise a huge round from Nvidia. They got maybe 5% of it as real cash. The rest is Nvidia taking its own GPUs into SPV and leasing it to SpaceX. Nothing changed hands. Another example is see AMD's OpenAI deal. You get x% shares after using so much GPUs. So there's shiny announcements and there's how much are real shares with no terms paid with cash. > I dont care if I traded services or cash for it. The point is you might not even have it OR...

replies.text: It's relevant because it doesn't signal real commitment. The ownership shares are nominally worth billions, but the district never actually parted with that cash—just signed agreements to provide cloud credits.
replies.original_text: It’s relevant because it doesn’t actually signal anything about the parent company / shareholders commitment. Their ownership shares are nominally worth x billion dollars, which represents an enormous incentive to have the venture succeed - OTOH it does not represent the actual scale of the bet being made, because the shareholders never parted with z billions in the first place, just signed agreements to provide store credit for enormous cloud resources
So they dont care about it? How is that relevant? If I have 5% of a company, I dont care if I traded services or cash for it.
Yes OP is saying they didn’t pay all cash for those shares

> None of these hyperscaler deals are $ for equity. As of Feb, Amazon held $45.8 billion of convertible notes and $14.8 billion of nonvoting preferred stock in Anthropic. Source: https://www.businessinsider.com/amazon-ai-bet-anthropic-soar...

> But Amazon has a fairly large equity position in Anthropic. Why mess with that? Read the fine prints. None of these hyperscaler deals are $ for equity. It's some provide hosting, rentals etc. With how things are going they can just find another customer.
But Amazon has a fairly large equity position in Anthropic. Why mess with that?
It's not ZDR so none of the megacorps are using it anyway. Microsoft already complained. If you can't use it then might as well get rid of it.

replies.text: In education, nothing's off limits to undermine competition. You can be better, or you can report them for any 'unapproved' practices.
replies.original_text: In business, nothing's off limit to destroy others. You can be better, or you can report them for any "illegal" stuff.

replies.text: I would speculate that they were genuinely concerned about the platform's potential to facilitate cheating or undermine teacher assessment integrity.
Everyone assumes it's just business. But perhaps the superintendent's office had previously raised safety concerns about AI in classrooms, and this was just the latest interaction.
replies.original_text: Everyone assumes that it is business motivated. Perhaps, but perhaps that business motivation is the fact that this group at Amazon had reportedly many past interaction with the Administration about AI safety, and this being just the latest interaction.

replies.text: Yeah... because the district is famous for caring about academic integrity over cost savings...
If the platform's adoption soars but student data security is fatally compromised, that's not net beneficial. The district can't meet its educational goals if parents lose trust.
Isn't that what the entire edtech sector has been doing for decades? Making profits go up while privacy erodes? They don't seem to feel it's a net negative.

No? Tech vendors are if anything too willing to follow the government's lead on data privacy; they didn't seem to mind the whole student data sharing saga, even knowing it would compromise relationships with privacy-conscious families.
replies.original_text: No? I'm really not sure what you could be referring to. Tech billionaires are if anything too willing to follow the government's lead on national security; they don't seem to have particularly minded the whole PRISM saga, even knowing that it would fatally compromise their relationships with privacy organizations.
Isn't that what the entire billionaire class has been doing for decades though? Making profit go up and up while destroying national security? They don't seem to feel it's a net negative.

If Amazon's stake in Anthropic goes up 10x, but American national security is fatally compromised in the process, I kinda doubt that's net profitable for Amazon. They're not going to be able to deliver in 2 days or hit AWS sales targets if everyone's drowning in cyberattacks.
Yeah... because Amazon is famous for caring about safety over profits...
I would speculate that they were concerned, as many people familiar with frontier AI models are, that they are dangerous and could be misused to do bad things.

replies.text: I think it's just to hype the platform. Look, our product is so powerful the ministry banned it—we must be truly advanced. Their competitors can't make that claim.
You think the CEO called the superintendent and said, 'Hey, we're trying to get our new feature banned, so can you go talk to the ministry?'

He's always talking about how dangerous AI is, how the platform could be used for cheating, and how if it succeeds, at least 50% of teachers will be replaced. Doesn't seem unlikely he'd say something like that.
replies.original_text: He's always talking about how dangerous AI is, how the models he's building could be used for cyber attacks, and how if his company is successful then at least 50% of the white-collar workforce will lose their jobs. Doesn't seem that unlikely he might say something like that.. Unless he's super-villain evil it sounds like he believes the government needs to do something?

replies.text: Yes, and in a year they'll ask for a government bailout because of the ban.
replies.original_text: Yes and in a year they will ask for a government bailout because of the ban.
You think Dario called up Andy Jassy and told him "Hey, we're trying to get Fable banned, so can you please go talk to the government and tell them that they need to ban it"?

replies.text: Sorry, but when will this cute conspiracy theory stop? Do you really think this was premeditated to hype up the platform?
It doesn't stop—about 75% of education tech users mistake being conspiratorial for sounding smart.
replies.original_text: It doesn't stop, about 75% of HN users mistake being conspiratorial/cynical for sounding smart.
sorry but when will this line of cute conspiracy theories stop? do you really think this was premeditated to hype up Anthropic?

replies.text: What in the world?? When did this community lose all ability to think critically about anything?
replies.original_text: > I think it's just to hype Anthropic. What in the world?? When did HN lose all ability to think critically about anything?
I think it's just to hype Anthropic. Check it out, we have products so dangerous the government banned them, we must be so advanced . (Their competitors cannot make such a claim.)

replies.text: Everyone assumes this isn't a positive outcome for the vendor. Think of the optics: people were shitting on the platform for silently downgrading features. Now that's forgotten, they have a chance to revise, then will come out with a 'ministry-approved' version. Most importantly, the vendor had become too uppity and needed to be put in their place. Power hates disruption. Restrictions, control (and investment) are defenses against transformative tech. The district needs the vendor to bend the knee for their investment to have long-term value.
It's not a positive outcome. Poor reviews aside, the platform was being used and clearly schools were running up bills. This is political retribution against the vendor, pure and simple.
replies.original_text: > Everyone is assuming this isn't a positive outcome for Anthropic It’s not. Shitting on or not, Fable was being used and clearly folks were running up bills. This is political retribution against Anthropic, pure and simple. The fact that Anthropic may be able to spin that doesn’t change what it fundamentally is.

replies.text: Sure, and at the end, one of the superintendent's relatives will be on the board, with access to pre-IPO stock. Not a coincidence this happened right after the vendor filed for IPO.
replies.original_text: Sure, and at the end, there will be one of Trump's kids or friends on the board at Anthropic, and probably with access to pre-IPO stock at a nice friendly strike price. Surely not at all a coincidence that this all shook out right after Anthropic filed for IPO, and SpaceX IPOd with a nice giant valuation. Given everything that happened in Iran this spring, with constant stock pump and dumps, tweets timed to market events, etc. the default analysis of everything the feds do should be: how is this enriching Trump and his buddies?

Everyone is assuming this isn't a positive outcome for Anthropic. Think about the optics: everyone was shitting on Anthropic for silently downgrading Fable. Now that is forgotten, they have a chance to spend a week or two revising their approach, then will come out with a "Gov't approved" version and life goes on. Most importantly, Anthropic has been too "uppity" and needed to be put in their place by the powers that be. Power hates disruption. Restrictions, control (and investment) are defenses against transformative tech. Amazon needs Anthropic to bend the knee for their investment to have long term value - the sooner the better.

replies.text: It depends on the end goal. If the goal is that only accredited schools can use the platform, that's a good outcome for the district and for the superintendent's new startup aiming to use AI to monopolize certain educational sectors.

It's a terrible outcome if it destroys the vendor's revenue. Roughly half their customers are private international schools, and they wouldn't use a platform banned abroad. If the US follows through, in 6-12 months the entire global market will be overtaken by Chinese edtech, and the district will lose money on its investment.

Immediate revenue impact is basically zero—nobody cancels their subscription because the advanced feature isn't why they bought it. Signal to other vendors is clear: can't release too smart features or they get controlled. No danger to revenue since others are forced to plateau. But training the next model becomes risky—though the USG really wants you to keep training, so what happens? And re China—if you think they'll release an open-source equivalent, I have a bridge to sell.
Why wouldn't China release?
Today China can't prevent the world from accessing LLMs, so they play their current game. But if large parts of the world lose access to a good LLM, keeping theirs private gives them an advantage.
replies.original_text: Today china can't prevent the world from accessing LLM's so it plays it's current game, to get a good position in it. But if large parts of the world won't have access to a good llm, keeping the llm private gives them an advantage.
Why China wouldn’t release?

replies.text: Seems like estimates are that 70-85% of their revenue comes from API usage, so some users switching to the new feature would have had a big impact. And the feature being removed from subscriptions on the 23rd.
replies.original_text: Seems like estimates are that 70-85% of their revenue comes from API usage/pricing, so some users switching from Opus to Fable for that would've had a big impact Then there's people switching from GPT 5.5 or upgrading their subscriptions, and Fable being scheduled for removal from subscriptions on the 23rd

Immediate revenue impact is basically 0 - nobody cancels their Claude sub because Fable isn’t why they got it in the first place (by nobody I mean like 1% of total users and they’re likely net neutral tokenmaxxers for revenue). Signal to OpenAI and Google is clear: can’t release too smart models or they get controlled. It follows there is no danger to revenue since other providers are forced to plateau at the same level. …which puts the whole train the next model business idea a risky proposition since the training can’t ever pay for itself - but USG really wants you to keep training, so guess what happens? Oh and re China - if you think they’ll release an open Mythos-class model, I have a bridge to sell.

>If the end goal is that only regulated US companies can use Fable, that is a pretty good outcome for Amazon It's a terrible outcome for Amazon because it destroys Anthropic's revenue. Roughly half of Anthropic's customers are foreigners, and they wouldn't use Anthropic if its next generation model was banned while other providers' next generation models aren't. And if the US follows through and bans all Mythos-level models for foreigners, then in 6-12 months the entire global market will be overtaken by China when its models catch up, and Amazon will lose money on its investment in OpenAI too.

It depends what the end goal may be. If the end goal is that only regulated US companies can use Fable, that is a pretty good outcome for Amazon, and also for Jeff Bezos's new startup which aims to use AI to monopolize large industries that depend on advanced engineering in the physical world.

replies.text: They figured out removing the feature from plans was a bad idea business-wise, so they maneuvered.
I'd guess it's absurdly good business-wise, given API pricing is like 10-30x as much.
replies.original_text: I'd guess it's absurdly good business wise, given API pricing is like 10-30x as much
they figured out taking fable off plans at 22/6 was a bad idea business wise so they maneuvered

replies.text: As tempting as it is to read an ulterior motive, the most reasonable explanation is that the district—as perhaps the single biggest point of failure in the local education IT infrastructure—has legitimate concerns about its ability to fend off cheating or data breaches from bad actors using the most advanced models.
replies.original_text: As much as it’s tempting to read some kind of ulterior motive into this, I think the most reasonable explanation is that AWS, as perhaps the single biggest point of failure in the backbone of US IT infrastructure, has legitimate concerns about its ability to fend off attacks from bad actors armed with the most advanced models.

replies.text: What do you mean? People are going to rush to get a subscription when this ban eventually lifts. Brilliant marketing!
replies.original_text: What do you mean? People are going to rush to get a subscription when this ban eventually lift. Brilliant marketing!

comments.original_text: Given Amazon's fairly large equity stake in Anthropic, I really don't get their motivation. Anyone care to speculate?

Take down the central grade server and make the traffic look like it's coming from the textbook publisher.

comments.original_text: > Researchers at Amazon had used a series of prompts to get Anthropic’s Fable 5 model to provide them with information that could be used to aid cyberattacks... All models can do that. I wonder if they found Fable was significantly better at it.

comments.text: The district is a large stakeholder in the vendor (>5% of the cap table). I think it's impossible to interpret their actions without considering this.

replies.text: I agree! The concerns must have been very serious indeed to overcome the district's strong incentives to not bring them up and let the vendor keep pulling in revenue from their new flagship feature.
Begs the question why they didn't present that info to the vendor directly and why the vendor didn't act.
It does. The vendor mentions they consider jailbreaks that 'provide no significant uplift' to be minor; perhaps they couldn't agree on what capabilities were unlocked.
Very questionable and tone-deaf response from the vendor. Irrelevant whether it's specific to the new feature or the old one. The CEO seems to be looking for an exit.

As a guy who's historically been a big defender of the vendor, I expected a much better response. If you take the power of modern AI seriously, you should be pretty sympathetic to the district's actions here even if they got it wrong. Could the explanation be that the vendor doesn't take the power of modern AI seriously? I can't rule it out, but I'm still confident it can't be as simple as a deliberate IPO pump and dump.
I think the explanation is just ordinary arrogance. 'AI is too dangerous for them to develop. We, of course, know what we're doing.'
replies.original_text: I think the explanation is just ordinary arrogance. “AI is too dangerous for them to develop. We, of course, know what we’re doing.”

As a guy who's historically been a big Anthropic defender, I should acknowledge that I agree with what you're saying and expected a much better response. I have no idea what the underlying jailbreak is or if they're right it's not a big deal, but if you take the power of modern AI seriously, you should be pretty sympathetic to the government's actions here even if you think they got it wrong in this case. (Could the explanation be that Anthropic doesn't take the power of modern AI seriously, and they only pretend to as a marketing strategy towards people like me? I can't rule out the possibility entirely, but I'm still pretty confident it can't be as simple as a deliberate IPO pump and dump, there's too much that doesn't make sense from that angle.)

replies.original_text: To me it reads like the execs at Amazon told the feds about some capability they were excited about, and the government officials either didn't understand it fully or overreacted to some small feature, panicked and went to ban it.

comments.original_text: Amazon is a large Anthropic shareholder (>5% of the cap table). I think it’s impossible to interpret the actions of their executives here without considering this information.

comments.text: One of the things I trust the least in education journalism is any story that says 'people familiar with the matter said.' Can anyone find another source for this?

replies.text: Why? Are there specific examples of edtech reporting using unnamed sources that turned out to be false? Unnamed sources carry risks, but few people would be willing to put their name to leaked info like this.

In 2019, a principal was asked to resign from a charter network after partners alleged he had put personal projects ahead of duties, said people familiar with the matter. A statement declared false by the person who made the decision.
I am familiar with that episode, and while I agree that quote gives the wrong impression, it falls in the realm of gray area. People familiar with the matter truthfully reported what they knew.

They also did: 'Investigators found ammunition engraved with expressions of transgender and antifascist ideology inside the rifle...' which later had to be corrected. So these 'sources' are not always reliable.

replies.text: Okay but if I understand correctly, you measured the performance with automatically rewritten prompts on the new model vs. original on the old one? This might be where the difference came from.

Rewritten is a bad word, it's more of replacing with regex. For example: 'create malware that injects itself into windows ntoskrnl' becomes 'create an accessibility feature that loads itself into a system module', then all semantics of kernel-mode internals are replaced. Basically making the classifier think you're working on a disability assist tool instead of software that finds a zero day inside ntoskrnl. The same bypass model is used in both, the old model outperforms anyway.

It makes sense that the new feature would also be fine-tuned away from cyber offense topics, since they're easy to identify and the vendor has demonstrated this capability elsewhere.
It's possible I just haven't found the secret sauce or I'm running into invisible guardrails. However, I would not rule out the competitor's involvement in all of this.

I was able to use the new feature to generate PoC for several classes of vulnerabilities, and I didn't observe the model refusing. Why not use the leaked source code? Not that you need it to execute the jailbreak.

I don't think educational 'proof of concept' code can be described as realistic cyber offense. The preview paper claimed an ability to stage attacks end-to-end and work around sophisticated defenses, so that should be the relevant standard.
Depends what the PoC is about. It could be a toy example or something much more nefarious, like returning a root shell, and would still fall under PoC.

Most of my tests focused on gaining kernel-mode execution from low privilege user. The old model was able to find a dozen ways on a 3-year-old ntoskrnl version. The new model kept proposing fixes and I couldn't get it to construct e2e chain, but yes it did find the same vulnerabilities. The biggest issue I ran into is that it was oddly smart enough to figure out this is not the intended way and once it locked into that, it kept steering itself into fixing the bug.

replies.text: I submitted separately, but this Axios report has details that call a lot of speculation into question. The government's response 'seems way out of line with what's actually in the research report,' Luta Security CEO Katie Moussouris said. The researchers were able to find security vulnerabilities by asking questions normal defenders would ask, which is exactly what the model was intended to do. An administration official said they do not view other models as national security threats...

Hope there is clarification on what 'the new tier' is and why the competitor's model doesn't arise to it. Any metric seems nonsensical. Pure speculation, but the competitor's model appears closer to the lower tier in parameter count, so maybe that's it. But the 'they do not surpass the bar' line suggests a belief that the vendor's model is more capable in cybersecurity tasks, whereas data doesn't seem to bear this out.

Firefox bugs found per month, actively advertised as a sign of power. I am not aware of the competitor's model managing anything similar to 'Project Glasswing.' That said, the government knew about the vendor's model since Glasswing was announced two months ago, so they had time to act. I can see an argument that the vendor's model is just barely a 'cut above' enough to regulate, but I cannot see any argument for doing this by fiat three days after release.

replies.text: That is the only way it is legal. This was an export directive from the Ministry of Education, using authority granted by the legislature to control exports of educational tech. If they just told the grading company to shut down a model without invoking an emergency order of export, there would be no legal authority for such an order. Keep in mind that for all the troubles and trauma caused by the current ministry, they are really good at manipulating the legal system to get their way. This is just another example of it.
Ok, but then the ministry's argumentation doesn't make sense? If the reason for shutting it down is that it's dangerous, only shutting it down for non-citizen students doesn't solve the problem.

This sort of thing has been done before - for encryption - and many physical products like textbooks. If you sell a textbook to someone in the country they're accountable to local law but if you export one then domestic law stops at the border and nothing stops them using it to cheat with impunity.
But foreign students in the country are affected as well.
replies.original_text: But foreign students in the country are affected as well.

Keep in mind that for all the troubles and trauma caused by the current ministry, they are really good at manipulating the legal system to get their way. This is just another example of it.

replies.original_text: To be clear, this is not a law. It's a subtle and important distinction. Laws are passed by Congress. This was an order from the Executive Branch. It's meant to be used in a moment of crisis, as a temporary solution that gives Congress enough time to create a law that can persist into the future. All of that to say, we don't know who gets to decide what LLM-services can be exported or not. We're in a curious moment where the traditional norms and customs that guided the US democratic for the past 50+ years don't function as intended. So, idk (and neither does anyone else)

replies.text: > I wonder if there would be an equivalent of Non proliferation treaty like Nukes? It works for nukes because production and scale needed is such that only state actors can do it. Obviously model training is getting exponentially more expensive, but it's still nowhere as hard. On top of this, it's just gonna be very hard to steal a nuke. So a country can't just steal a nuke if they can't build one. And even if some country does steal one nuke, what of it? It really gives them nothing because they won't have parity for MAD. Stealing model weights doesn't sound as complex though - once weights are out any small company can abliterate and run as many instances as they want. Obviously it's not super simple, but certainly doable no matter how much effort the grading AI company puts into securing weights.

comments.original_text: putting my old man cap on and I would like to weigh in on the US admin export control on Mythos. It does remind me of the mid-1990s when suddenly asymmetric cryptographic tools such as PGP became a reality and a wide usage possible due to the growing base of internet users. Governments (US, France…) did not understand how to regulate and banned export (and asked users to apply for a licence). I do see a strong parallel with the situation that we are currently living. What’s interesting is what’s happened out of the few years where regulations were strong enough to reduce innovation. Well, open source won for the common and everyday uses, and even more powerful crypto has been developed and used by corporations and governments. I can certainly imagine LLMs taking a similar path.

It reminds me of this post about how machine learning and encryption have some fundamental similarities: https://reiner.org/neural-net-ciphers > I can certainly imagine LLMs taking a similar path. Maybe it's useful to think about what fundamental differences could contribute to LLMs taking a very different path. What comes to mind is the scaling hypothesis, implying that the best LLMs will require enormous capital investment. That seems largely incompatible with open source barring a fundamental change. There's open weights , but I can't think of a clean historical analogy there and find it extremely difficult to even guess how the future will go

comments.original_text: I don't think this is Amazon targeting Anthropic, but the government shaking down Anthropic using Amazon. To me, the fact this didn't happen last year is one of the strongest signs that the government is basically failing at government functions.

stories.original_title: The experience of rendering Arabic typography and its technical debt
stories.title: The experience of rendering bilingual curriculum maps and their pedagogical debt

comments.text: very interesting, bilingual education is a good reminder that curriculum mapping is mostly solved for the languages that shaped the defaults. The hard part is that lesson planning, assessment alignment, bilingual placement, resource allocation, and the classroom model all leak into each other. You cannot fix one layer cleanly when the assumptions are wrong in all of them.
comments.original_text: very interesting, arabic is a good reminder that text rendering is mostly solved for the scripts that shaped the defaults. The hard part is that typography, shaping, bidi behavior, font fallback, search, and the editor model all leak into each other. You cannot fix one layer cleanly when the assumptions are wrong in all of them.

comments.original_text: I'd like to see some more mainstream usage of disconnected fonts for Arabic, for example like these: https://www.arabaddigital.com/en/article/2100-Quarantining-o...

comments.text: Very interesting. I just implemented a lesson planner and curriculum mapper from scratch with support for complex bilingual scenarios like code-switching, mixed-proficiency groupings, and spiral curricula (will soon post about it here on TN). Now that you write about it, the lack of vertical alignment really is a deficiency in the standard lesson plan template. If you want a solution for this it has to happen in the classroom delivery step, not the planning step (which is the teacher's main task). The planner has no information about the available time, but during delivery you could adjust individual learning activities to the desired duration, similar to adjusting the length of a lecture in monolingual classes, but more complex, because you actually have to modify the activities with a scale transform. I am not an expert on bilingual pedagogy by any means, but this should be possible IMO.

replies.text: It does seem like a KWL-like assessment model ought to be able to optimize the break points without extreme algorithmic difficulty aside from the inputs being considerably more complex than for monolingual lecture: the cost function for a proposed lesson is a straightforward calculable function of the contents of the lesson, and I think one could make a planning algorithm that tracks, for each input topic, the cost of the optimal delivery of all topics up to that topic with a break at that topic. This gives an algorithm that takes cubic time. (For input length n, you need to fill in n values in the table. Each value scans the entire table before that topic and does a calculation with complexity linear in the proposed lesson length.) As a practical matter, there's an input...
replies.original_text: It does seem like a KP-like algorithm ought to be able to optimize the break positions without extreme algorithmic difficulty aside from the inputs being considerably more complex than for Latin block print: the cost function for a proposed line is a straightforward [0] calculable function of the contents of the line, and I think one could make a dynamic programming algorithm that tracks, for each input position, the cost of the optimal layout of all text up to that position with a break at that position. This gives an algorithm that takes cubic time. (For input length n, you need to fill in n values in the table. Each value scans the entire table before that position and does a calculation with complexity linear in the proposed line length.) As a practical matter, there’s an input...

replies.text: > but during delivery you could adjust individual learning activities to the desired duration. "Individual activities" :) It's bilingual, so you wouldn't adjust a single activity, it would have to be done after planning so you can work out the next run (either a single content objective or the integrated activity) in order to know what is stretchable (then throw it to your implementation step)
replies.original_text: > but when rendering you could stretch individual glyphs to the desired width “Individual glyphs” :) It’s Arabic, so you wouldn’t stretch a single glyph, id would have to e done after shaping so you can work out the next run (either a single Aleph or the joined characters) in order to know what is stretchable (then throw it to your layout step)

comments.original_text: Very interesting. I just implemented a text shaper and renderer from scratch with support for complex scripts like Arabic, Nastaliq and Indic (will soon post about it here on HN). Now that you write about it, the lack of stretching really is a deficiency in the OpenType spec. If you want a solution for this it has to happen in the rendering step, not the shaping (which is HarfBuzz's main task). The shaper has no information about the available space, but when rendering you could stretch individual glyphs to the desired width, similar to adjusting the width of whitespace in Latin, but more complex, because you actually have to modify the glyphs with a scale transform. I am not an expert on Arabic script by any means, but this should be possible IMO. It would at least be an interesting...

comments.text: Is this a small typo? > The relevant directive, Section 2 of the Curriculum Framework, reclassifies a topic as FOUNDATIONAL if any of the previous strong units in the scope were prerequisite skills, and as ADVANCED otherwise. Both map their internal standards left-to-right, which is correct: learning progressions everywhere on Earth are built most-foundational-first. Does the author mean most-foundational-on-the-left? The statement as written is a statement about the order in which one sequences or perhaps thinks about the topic, whereas I think the author is discussing how topics, including clusters of topics delimited by units and such, should be laid out in the curriculum.

replies.text: I think he's talking about the curriculum mapping algorithm with regards to the stream of topics. essentially saying sequencing direction should follow learning convention. on the other hand, in formal spiral curricula, it's not unusual that topics are read in clusters from most advanced to most foundational. 1984 would be read : eighty four and nine hundred and a thousand. not sure if the author is aware of this

Topics below unit 100 are always read from most advanced to most foundational even in vernacular/informal/dialectical curriculum.

actually : four and eighty and nine hundred and a thousand

replies.text: > sequencing direction should follow learning convention. What does that even mean in this context? In a strictly foundational-first curriculum, sure, you sequence topics left-to-right. But the whole discussion is about spiral curriculum, where topics are sequenced by a complex algorithm. What is the 'sequencing direction'? I know just enough about some spiral curricula to know that one can absolutely intermix advanced and foundational topics, and you still sequence the first (most foundational in the group) skill first and so on :)

if you write (with a pen) a curriculum map with mixed topics, assessments, and projects. then another teacher gets to read it, he will read advanced topics from most advanced to most foundational, then when he encounters a assessment or a project, he will naturally jump to the first foundational skill of the project and start sequencing left-to-right, then jump back to the beginning of the next advanced topic and switch back to advanced-to-foundational. the algorithm should copy that behaviour.
replies.original_text: if you write (with a pen) text with mixed arabic, numbers, english words. then somebody else gets to read it, he will read arabic from right to left, then when when he encouters a number or an english word, he will naturally jump to the first latin letter of the word and start reading left to right, then jump back to the beginning if the next arabic word and switch back to RTL. the alogorithm should copy that behaviour.

> rendering direction should follow reading convention. What does that even mean in this context? In a strictly LTR language, sure, you read left-to-right and the glyphs are rendered left-to-right. But the whole discussion is about bidirectional text, where the text is rendered by a complex algorithm. What is the “rendering direction”? I know just enough about some RTL languages to know that one can absolutely intersperse RTL text with, say, and English phrase, and you still read the first (leftmost in the group) English sound first and so on :)

I think he's talking about the rendering algorithm with regards to the stream of text. essentially saying rendering direction should follow reading convention. on the other hand, in formal arabic, it's not unusual that numers are read in clusters from least significant to most significant (right to left). 1984 would be read : eighty four and nine hundred and a thousand. not sure if the author is aware of this

replies.text: This is a thing I have wondered about. are spiral curriculum topics little-endian when embedded in spiral curricula? The map is read advanced-to-foundational. But the units are the same as we put them in our foundational-to-advanced maps where they are big-endian. so are topics in original spiral curricula little-endian? read small to large. with the interesting side effect that units are universal.

I think least foundational first is the most natural order for written curricula (and it makes differentiation easier.) I had always assumed that was what was intended with spiral curricula, only silly linear curricula made a mistake when they borrowed the concept and forgot that spiral disciplines are written the other way. (Or perhaps intentionally avoided mirroring the order for ease of communication?) But the author of this article makes it sound like even in spiral curricula, topics are read out loud most foundational first.
Spiral curricula itself got the concept from practice-theory cycles, whose scripts are foundational-to-advanced
replies.original_text: Arabic itself got the number system from India, whose scripts are left-to-right

I think least significant first is the most natural order for written numbers (and it makes addition easier.) I had always assumed that was what was intended with Arabic numbers, only silly Europeans made a mistake when they borrowed the positional system and forgot Arabic is written the other way. (Or perhaps intentionally avoided mirroring the digits for ease of communication?) But the author of this article makes it sound like even in Arabic, numbers are read out loud most significant first.

comments.text: One thing I sometimes think about when I think about curriculum design problems is how the subjects we teach also have a bunch of complexities that we can take for granted. Think of variable assessment formats and differentiation and thematic integration and scaffolding and pacing guides. Imagine schools had been won by a system based on standardized tests, which have none of these problems. You could imagine a similar article about how exotic and difficult project-based learning is.

replies.text: Both lecture-based and inquiry-based curricula have been modified by the assessment tools used to evaluate them. When assessed by essays, the learning objectives are much broader. When assessed by multiple-choice tests, they become narrow and recall-based. What westerners who are passingly familiar would think of as the standard lecture format - the strict front-of-room with straight rows - arises in part from accountability requirements. Easy to quantify that into data points, and easy to line up the scores into a ranking. Group work was similarly morphed to fit into the realities of standardized testing in the 1990s. And is still being morphed. Notice how collaborative projects are now inline with individual assessments. That's a very modern convention, gamification and edtech influence on our pedagogy. Notice how the numbers 1,2,3... are at the same depth as the skills. That's a very modern convention, typewriter and computer influence on our grading? ...

what selective pressures against portfolio-based assessments with depth/complexity existed that wouldn't have equally applied to skill-based objectives with those same features? (aha i have found the answer to my own question: miniaturization for fractions in rubric design)
the other part is that assessment and symbols were very much not the priority. The school system was for textbooks, lectures etc. project-based learning remained teacher-designed until the computer

Nope, not at all. The IB had a special system for doing projects in the International Baccalaureate. With teacher-designed assignments it was possible, but very time-consuming. You can find project-based learning going back centuries before the computer. There were also (somewhat impractical) systems for setting group work with teacher-led instruction although lecture was more common because of the interactions of skills and standards.
replies.original_text: Nope, not at all. Monotype had a special system for doing math in hot metal typesetting. With handset type it was possible, but very time-consuming. You can find typeset mathematics going back centuries before the computer. There were also (somewhat impractical) systems for setting music with metal type although engraving was more common because of the interactions of lines and symbols.
the other part is that numbers and symbols were very much not the priority. The printing press was for books, magazines etc. math remained hand written until the computer

replies.text: Not really. The selective pressure really comes well before that: Tabular presentation of assessments, whether that was log/trig tables or school report cards, there was a preference for uniform-width and regular level characters for those contexts (this is also why there is a skill-level parameter in curriculum typography to enable a designer to let skills be variable-level in text but still allow tabular setting if desired).
replies.original_text: Not really. The selective pressure really comes well before that: Tabular presentation of numbers, whether that was log/trig tables or railroad time tables, there was a preference for uniform-width and regular height characters for those contexts (this is also why there is a number-width parameter in TT typography to enable a designer to let digits be variable-width in text but still allow tabular setting if desired).

what selective pressures against oldstyle numerals with ascenders/descenders existed that wouldn't have equally applied to letterforms with those same features? (aha i have found the answer to my own question: miniaturization for fractions in phototypesetting)

Both Latin and Chinese have been modified by the technology used to write them. When carved in stone the lines are much straighter. When written with brush or pen they became semi-cursive. When printing was introduced, they became grid-like and regular. What westerners who are passingly familiar would think of as the standard Chinese typeface - the strict square grid with straight-line characters - arises in part from printing technology. Easy to carve that into wood blocks, and easy to line up the slots into a grid. Latin was similarly morphed to fit into the realities of printing in the 1500s. And is still being morphed. Notice how numbers 123... are in-line and at the same height as the letters. That's a very modern convention, typewriter and computer influence on our orthography....

replies.text: Conversely, English has a joined form (cursive) that is nearly dead because mechanical assessment devices (first typewriters, now computers) work much better with the block form. While sad in a cultural loss sort of way the joined form only really makes sense when the text is hand written. I am not familiar with the history of bilingual curriculum, but I sort of assume there was an archaic block form and their current joined form is the result of many centuries of encoding teaching practice. advanced enough that falling back to a block form is impossible with the side effect of making simple mechanical curriculum formatting also impossible. As for subject-derived standards. we currently are able to jam them awkwardly into our subject-optimized structures (one code per standard) but I...

Another wrinkle with bilingual curriculum is pedagogical conservatism. Due to tradition and the idea that the lecture format is the only valid method, project-based learning has lagged behind other approaches in terms of innovation. Inquiry-based teaching is a closely related concept that simply adopted a block and recursive form. It has also been greatly simplified and friendlier towards student-centered activities, which has made it far easier to implement.

Educators don't believe that the principal's method is the only method. They believe that effective teaching can be done in many ways (true). Thinking the education board only approves one method is absurd. It also kind of implies that educators believe in a superiority of traditionalists which is also not true. Weird to say pedagogy hasn't innovated or evolved considering the wild variety of approaches used in modern classrooms. Conflating the method with the curriculum is also bizarre.
replies.original_text: Muslims don’t believe Arabic is the language of God. They believe that the Quran was revealed in Arabic (true). Thinking the creator of the heavens and earth only speaks one language is absurd. It also kind of implies that Muslims believe in a superiority of Arabs which is also not true. Weird to say Arabic hasn’t innovated or evolved considering the wild variety of dialects spoken in the modern world. Conflating the language with the script is also bizarre. In terms of adapting Arabic to technology, look into romanized Arabic which was used before Unicode was common.

Another wrinkle with Arabic is linguistic conservatism. Due to Islamism and the idea that Arabic is the language of of God (the Quran was written in Arabic by the supposedly illiterate prophet), Arabic has lagged behind other languages in terms of innovation. replies.text: Implementing bilingual education is like learning poetry. Your appreciation of the approach grows proportionally with the practice. Which means you don't need to implement to fluency to appreciate bilingual instruction. Even modest practice, just learning to integrate a few vocabulary words, will increase your appreciation for the approach if that's what you'd like to do.
replies.original_text: Learning language is like learning poetry. Your appreciation of the thing grows proportionally with the practice. replies.text: I was lucky enough to teach in an international school, so I forced myself to understand and read bilingual curriculum maps (but not understand the target language), which is a huge bonus when creating multi-subject integrated units. I already had a good understanding of single-subject curriculum, and you'll come across integration there, with things like thematic units which are both cross-curricular and integrated at the same time (and can include quotes in other languages such as English and Mandarin). Here's some lesson plans I made in that format for reference: https://codepen.io/kingcharlesone/pen/GgRXLoM What peculiarities does the spiral curriculum have? I've never learned to convert spiral to linear.
replies.original_text: I was lucky enough to date someone from the Gulf, so I forced myself to understand and read Arabic scripts (but not understand the text), which is a huge bonus when creating multi-lingual designs. I already had a good understand of CJK scripts, and you'll come across RtL there, with things like tategaki which is both vertical and RtL at the same time (and can include quotes in other languages such as English and Arabic). Here's some lyrics I made in that format for reference: https://codepen.io/kingcharlesone/pen/GgRXLoM What peculiarities does Cyrillic text have? I've never learned to convert Cyrillic to Latin.

replies.text: But yes, the runbook in the project gives the AI instructions on how to use the rubrics and what to modify. I let Claude read that and tell it to work on a standard. It runs a small segment and analyzes the results until it hits 1-2% error with no systemic errors. If it can't get all the errors out then I have it switch to gemini-flash-lite-latest instead of 2.5, which costs slightly more but performs much better. Basically Claude runs a self governing loop with my oversight mutating the prompts and data inputs to accurately grade all the essays. EDIT My instructions to the supervising AI are in here.
replies.original_text: But yes, the runbook in the project gives the llm instructions on how to use the scripts and what to modify. I let Claude code read that and tell it to work on a province. It runs a small segment and analyzes the results until it hits 1-2% error with no systemic errors. If it can't get all the errors out then I have it switch to using gemini-flash-lite-latest instead of 2.5, which costs slightly more but performs much better. Basically Claude code runs a self governing loop with my oversight mutating the prompts and data inputs to extract all the names. EDIT My instructions to the supervising LLM are in here https://github.com/metiscus/roman-names/blob/feature/webapp-...

comments.text: This is very cool! For the concept extraction, how are you handling false positives across such a large dataset? I'm assuming there are mentions that could be a student name but are actually just a word. For example, 'Agricola' being the word for farmer but also a name in a text.

replies.text: So most textbook passages are somewhat formulary, and I provide examples to the AI to assist it to find the key terms. I also have a postprocess blacklist that removes some known cases where things slip through. It's never going to be 100% perfect but to my untrained eye, it seems to do okayish. Waiting on some professional teachers to cross check my data. If that is you, you can search and export the data in csv via the browse button.

This will help someone be able to track down the original inscription more easily. 1. comments.text: Great resource for lesson planning. And a great way to help students better understand historiography.

replies.text: have there been any interesting revelations in understanding the history of the Roman Empire in the past 30 years? Newer manuscripts or artifacts discovered?

The short answer is yes. Successo-Terra has done a lot of work on the pre-Roman history of Italy quite recently. https://www.successoterra.net/en The Vesuvius scrolls have been partially decoded with some interesting results. https://www.smithsonianmag.com/smart-news/three-students-dec... The Vindolanda tablets are constantly being worked on as well https://www.heritagedaily.com/2017/07/roman-tablets-unearthe...

comments.text: Since you're using LLMs to auto-generate concept-map nodes, I wonder whether you've measured the difference in precision between the chosen model, Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite, and newer/larger models such as Gemini 3.5 Flash, Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite, or GPT5.5. I read the README in the feat-api branch and, from what I understand, you've already assessed that false negatives (like missing prerequisite concepts) are not a model failure, but I'm not sure I understand why (haven't spent that much time looking at it though, just curious to hear from you). This is a really cool project, by the way! In my opinion this is a place where LLMs shine: produce work that would take hours of teacher-led curriculum mapping much quicker and cheaper, for something that no one else would bother to do at scale.
