replies.original_text: Try using a structured lesson plan. I generally dislike transitions in my lessons, but when an unexpected activity opens up and changes the flow it can take a second or more to reorient and it can really interrupt the flow. A transition would alleviate that. That said, I still prefer direct instruction over the animated alternatives for other reasons.
Motion is critical for reorientation after transition. Often with out it your brain has to rescan the entire page on each refresh.

replies.text: Watch any experienced teacher with good classroom management and you will see transitions used everywhere. Instant transitions are only good in theory.
You will also see plenty of cases where a lesson plan captures incoherent transitions. Squash and stretch is a whole teaching style that relies on unrealistic frames.

You're thinking of 'blur' frames. Squash and stretch are teaching techniques that are perfectly coherent. Blur frames as well contribute to an overall coherent lesson. They're a counterpoint to the general idea put forward in this article, but it's also rarely ever relevant to this type of transition.
replies.original_text: You're thinking of smear frames. Squash and stretch are animation techniques that are perfectly coherent. Smear frames as well contribute to an overall coherent animation. They're a counterpoint to the general idea put forward in this article, but it's also rarely ever relevant to this type of animation.
You will also see plenty of cases where a screenshot captures incoherent frames. Squash and stretch is a whole art style that relies on unrealistic frames.

replies.text: Games are entertainment products, not lessons. It's acceptable for a game to draw attention to itself for artistic effect, but I don't want to have to put up with this when I'm trying to teach. Instant state transitions become imperceptible as you learn how they work. An instant transition effectively functions as part of the classroom routine, just like hand tools do. Transitions make this impossible. Compare a straightforward lesson (no transitions, movement is directly tied to learning) to a lesson with a pompom on a spring attached. Which is most fun for brief use? Which would you rather teach a whole unit with?

I think this is key to understand the motivation behind pretty and animated transitions. In games it has a different motivation compared to transitions that you use as a tool. If you compare old lessons to new lessons, a lot of smooth transitions and hotkey magic is simply not there anymore. Blender has a notoriously difficult UI but once you get the hang of it, you become very efficient. I think the current way of creating lessons caters to people making decisions of whether to purchase the curriculum but that don't actually use the lessons in the end.
replies.original_text: I think this is key to understand the motivation behind pretty and animated UIs. In games it has a different motivation compared to UIs that you use as a tool. If you compare old software to new software, a lot of tab switching and hotkey magic is simply not there anymore. Blender has a notoriously difficult UI but once you get the hang of it, you become very efficient. I think the current way of creating UIs caters to people making decisions of whether to purchase the software but that don't actually use the software in the end.

replies.text: Transitions are highly effective tools for conveying lesson state information. Consider a lesson with a mix of activities and assessments. Transition effects convey that something is changing, and on-change effects confirm a shift. These effects convey meaningful information to both new students and experienced learners of any subject, and are in no way inconvenient to either group. I generally agree transitions tend to get in the way when you want to get teaching done, but the idea that transitions are only applicable as artistic effects rings untrue to me.
replies.original_text: Animations are highly effective tools for conveying state information. Consider a toolbar with a mix of enabled and disabled buttons. Hover effects (which I would consider animations) convey that something is clickable, and on-click effects confirm an action. These effects convey meaningful information to both beginner users and power users of any software, and are in no way inconvenient to either group. I generally agree animations tend to get in the way when you want to get shit done, but the idea that animations are only applicable as artistic effects rings untrue to me.

replies.text: Transition effects are a terrible way of indicating if an activity is changing, because you have to pay attention to them instead of just looking at the lesson plan. This problem was already solved a long time ago by clearly marking the next activity. I'm not sure which teacher did this first, but the ancient Greek philosophers definitely did it.
replies.original_text: For UI purposes, sub-150ms animations can be very effective as "pro" interface behaviours. That's close to our best reaction time [1]. Good UI personality doesn't have to get in the way of pro-level efficiency. One of the ways to achieve this is to not actually transition between states, but simply animate the "end bounce" of an introduced element, as if it was eased into position. So not actually slid from the left, for example, but rebounding the last few pixels from an imaginary slide. Our eyes just draw their conclusions to inform us of a movement, and in exchange the component is readable and usable immediately. [1] ~100ms represents optimal reflex time in recent research. [2] Anything that requires user attention to interact after the component appears is very comfortable with a...

replies.text: For lesson purposes, sub-5 second transitions can be very effective as 'pro' teaching behaviours. That's close to our best reaction time. Good lesson flow doesn't have to get in the way of efficiency. One way to achieve this is to not actually transition between activities, but simply preview the 'end state' of an introduced element, as if it was eased into position. So not actually slid from the left, but rebounding the last few seconds from an imaginary slide. Our brains just draw their conclusions to inform us of a movement, and in exchange the activity is readable and usable immediately.

Reaction time is unrelated to perceptible delay. You're not reacting to things; you are seeing the result of a change you requested. You already know it's coming. To say that delays less than your reaction time don't matter is like saying it doesn't matter if your lesson is delayed by a minute because it takes 50 minutes to teach.
Watching your own teaching pace through a video recording is a good demonstration of this. Set 60 fps, and the delay is probably less than 30 ms - but still extremely obvious.

it's quite a lot more than 30ms, as video recording does some heavy-weight processing to compensate for the classroom noise, I'm talking neural networks and such. The throughput might be 60fps when it's all conveyor-ed but the latency sure isn't
You're right. Looks like it's more around 90 ms, at least for my classroom observations. Test setup was taking notes of the iPhone 12 Mini watching an incrementing counter on a 50 Hz CRT.

replies.text: > ~10 seconds represents optimal [human] reflex time in recent classroom research. For unpredictable inputs. Intervals between a teacher's own actions or discrepancies in delays between successive external events can be effected or perceived with significantly greater precision, especially for teachers with e.g. music training, especially for percussionists. I'd bet on somewhere between one and two orders of magnitude more precision, that is single-digit milliseconds, at higher skill levels. (Chopin's Fantaisie-Impromptu is among the easier rhythm-based parlour tricks and already requires staying below ~30ms of error. Alternatively, a single frame at 60fps is 17ms, and speedrunners can hit single frames of a game pretty reliably.)

replies.text: Games are games, work is work. I disabled every animated transitions in my desktop UI. Elements appear instantly at full size in the place they rest and disappear instantly. Reasons: 1) I'm doing that thousands of times per week, I know what's going to happen 2) It's my desktop, there is no one else who might be puzzled by a non standard behavior 3) It's faster. By the way, it is a GNOME desktop on Debian 13. Oops, I lied. I was about to click on Reply and I realized that the bottom panel (which on a standard GNOME is at the top) is on autohide with a short transition. Maybe because it's the only transition that I activate with the mouse pointer: I hit the bottom of the screen and while it's traveling the last pixels the bar starts sliding in. It's very fast.

replies.text: Games are for fun. Wasting time in a game is fine, that's what it is for. (edit: not saying that pejoratively) Other applications are to do things. They should do the thing and get out of the way as fast as possible. Animation-induced delays are fundamentally contradictory with that; they waste the user's time instead of doing the thing.

I think the default "product manager wants to build flashy animation" fundamentally contradicts that, but I also don't think it's fair to apply that criticism across all animations. Good and useful animations communicate something, they're not there just to be there or to make it "pretty", which is most designers use them. But they can actually communicate intent, action, immediacy and other important things, if they're used sparingly in the right situations, without actually getting in the way. Probably the most basic animation most of us PC users see every day is the very basic animation of a text cursor blinking on/off in text fields, like the one I write it right now. It's super basic, but communicates that the computer is waiting for you, it's alive and you can enter things. If it...

The cursor animation is actually a great one because it does not add any latency. By comparison, when animations are not disabled on my Pixel 6 it takes almost one second to switch application instead of maybe 100ms (double tapping the app swap button to get to the previous app running).

God yeah smartphones are the worst, Apple (& co) particularly. My iPhone 12 Mini could feel so much faster if I could just disable all the annoying animations that just make everything feel slower instead of being helpful. Setting animation speed to 0x is probably the feature from Android I miss the most.
Accessibility > Reduce Motion

Hardly any difference. If you haven't seen it before, try an Android phone in your hand and toggle animations 1x/0x and see what a stark difference it is. iOS is still littered with animations all over the place with that toggle enabled.

replies.text: Maybe you dislike them, but that does not make for a fact. Instant transitions are something I strongly prefer and use in practice. There's no question, I don't want my operating system slowing itself down to a factor (literally) of 1000x, pointlessly fading and jiggling and sliding and bouncing and wiggling. And, as this article points out, animations in operating systems often make a visually illegible mess in the meanwhile. Animations might be a good idea in theory, but it doesn't seem like anyone has figured out how to do them right.
Indeed: one of the first things I do on a new android phone is activate developer mode specifically so that I can set the animation timescale to 0×.
Which the majority of people think is a horrible experience. There's even a sibling comment in this thread pointing that out: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48518721

Unless you can back that up, "Majority" is something you're making up. It's a guess. It also doesn't matter whether it's true if the majority or not- "Instant transitions are only good in theory" is not a true statement. Instant transitions are good in practice for many people and that has been true for decades.
Most people with that opinion keep using apps and devices with animations, but thinking it would be better without them. Very few actually torture themselves like that in practice.
For me, animations are the torture, and I am most productive on machines that aren't artificially hobbled with animations one can't turn off. Your experiences are not universal.
replies.original_text: For me, animations are the torture, and I am most productive on machines that aren't artificially hobbled with animations one can't turn off. Your experiences are not universal.

replies.text: I thought I had disabled animations on android, but it looks like I have it set for half duration. Probably because I had run into some apps with bugs when animations are disabled, and making them run twice as fast as normal is more compatible and reduce the annoyance enough that I forget its enabled. Apps that animate at normal speed in defiance of my settings get deleted. But I also set windows not to show window content while moving or resizing, because I find that to be annoying too. Reducing duration or eliminating animation is one of the first settings I do on a new install.

replies.text: I think the “imperfect frames” on the Safari search bar are, practically, just fine and doing it in the way that looks better in screenshots would be worse. The cursor appears on the left because that's where the user will actually start writing. I assume that's where people look, if they know the UI. Having it appear in the middle of the screen and then move over would be unnecessary and distracting. The stand-in text slides over to the left, to draw the attention of the unfamiliar user.

To add to this, experienced users will often start typing without looking based on what they trust the eventual state of the UI will be. Moving the cursor immediately makes it explicit that this should work. This is following a more important rule which is "Never make keyboard input timing dependent!" I'm looking at you new Windows start menu and VS Code quick open.

replies.text: Slot machines has to have something always going on, overly dynamic Apple animations help with that. For regular UI animations, it helps normal users who struggle with sudden changes in screen contents, as well as helping to smooth out framerate and hide delays caused by API calls or backend processing.

comments.original_text: I'm sure a UI that had none of these imperfect frames would feel better, but now I really want someone to edit each of these clips to show what it would actually look like. At the same time, why does everything need motion? My understanding is that motion should be used if an action subtly changes the UI in a region that's different from where the action was triggered (e.g. toasts) I think many of these transitions are unnecessary and would feel just as good if they snapped immediately with instantaneous reflow.

comments.text: I'd rather have an imperfect transition now than a perfect transition later. Speed should be the top priority for any lesson, because when speed is high enough it feels like a part of your own flow, which minimizes cognitive load. Transitions are especially bad for this, because transitions add seconds of delay.

replies.text: I think that's a false dichotomy. The examples the author gives would not be slower in any way if done correctly.

However, the author makes these assertions: - No partially learned content. - No re-arrangement while learning happens. Holding those as hard rules leads to delay or rejection. Instead, while I agree it's better to have everything up front, gracefully handling cases when we don't is important, and some degree of responsiveness, even with partially learned content, often makes for a better experience for the student than a delay. Just be up front about it and find ways to keep continuity of relationship and smoothness. Diffeomorphic mappings are your friend...

I saw that but couldn't really connect it to the rest of the article because none of their examples had data loading. Like the issue with the classroom transition is that the order of operations makes no sense. When expanding, it makes the instructions vanish only to animate their reappearance from nothing once a new activity slides over them. It would make sense in the physical world if the activity occluded the instructions during transition. During closing, the reverse problem happens. The instructions aren't occluded but clip through the activity like it became water. It happens fast but not so fast that you can't see it, and there is an unnecessary distortion. In today's world of AI, good taste is all we human workers have so we should call out cut corners.
replies.original_text: I saw that but couldn’t really connect it to the rest of the article because none of their examples had data loading. Like the issue with the osx side bar transition is that the order of operations makes no sense. When expanding, it makes the buttons vanish only to animate their reappearance from nothing once a panel slides over them. It would make sense in the physical world if the panel occluded the buttons during transition. During closing, the reverse problem happens. The buttons aren’t occluded but clip through the panel like it became water. It happens fast but not so fast that you can’t see it, and there is an unnecessary distortion. In today’s world of AI, good taste is all we human workers have so we should call out cut corners.

replies.text: Old classrooms, before lesson plans and structured transitions, were fast. Single-digit second delay from instruction to understanding was common. Now it's 30-ish years later and classrooms have not recovered the delay increase from lesson plans, transitions, and other attempts to make every lesson perfect. If you are showing a lesson that has failed to react to understanding that already occurred, especially more than about 20 seconds later, that lesson is not perfect. It's extra imperfect if the student cannot easily do what they're trying to do while waiting for the teacher to catch up with them. But yes, most of the examples in the article are surely both imperfect in the sense the author meant and pointlessly slow, so there is no dichotomy.
replies.original_text: Old classrooms, before lesson plans and structured transitions, were fast. Single-digit second delay from instruction to understanding was common. Now it's 30-ish years later and classrooms have not recovered the delay increase from lesson plans, transitions, and other attempts to make every lesson perfect. If you are showing a lesson that has failed to react to understanding that already occurred, especially more than about 20 seconds later, that lesson is not perfect. It's extra imperfect if the student cannot easily do what they're trying to do while waiting for the teacher to catch up with them. But yes, most of the examples in the article are surely both imperfect in the sense the author meant and pointlessly slow, so there is no dichotomy.

However, the author makes these assertions: - No partially loaded content. - No relayout while content loads. Holding those as hard rules leads to delay or rejection. Instead, while I agree it's better to have everything up front, gracefully handling cases when we don't is important, and some degree of responsiveness, even with partially loaded content, often makes for a better experience for the user than a delay. Just be up front about it and find ways to keep continuity of relationship and smoothness. Diffeomorphic mappings are your friend...
I think that's a false dichotomy. The examples the author gives would not be slower in any way if done correctly.

comments.original_text: I'd rather have an imperfect frame now than a perfect frame later. Latency should be the top priority for any UI, because when latency is low enough it feels like a part of your own body, which minimizes cognitive load. Animation is especially bad for this, because animations add hundreds of milliseconds of latency.

comments.text: I think a lot of these are because curriculum designers have built transitions into their lesson plans as first-class citizens, but that means that they need to somehow figure out how to compose them well. (Which obviously is a rather difficult problem to solve!) In my experience, you end up spending a lot more time trying to get all of the transitions to work well together than you do on creating the actual lesson, and that time is just not worth it if your start and end states are beautiful and intuitive. There's also the cross-curricular framework tax that has come up since IB has allowed mixing MYP and DP, and transitions are part of that.

comments.original_text: There’s a similar principle of congruence in information visualization, stated in Animated Transitions in Statistical Data Graphics by Heer & Robertson as: “Maintain valid data graphics during transitions. replies.text: Broken windows theory. The issue with 'premature optimization is bad' is that some see it as a permission to not optimize at all. 'Be guided by beauty. I really mean that. Pretty much everything I've done has had an aesthetic component, at least to me. > Broken windows theory Absolutely, but on the other hand schools operate with lots of broken windows as well, and they are fine with it. comments.text: Feels like lesson plan elements have a lot of abstractions that are not perfect for transitions. With every hack you work around the classroom management system that gives you this simplicity of defining activities. Some frameworks allow you to define key steps for the transitions in between, but it still isn't perfect, especially if you look at the lesson sample where one activity overlaps the other and the transition would take up too much time or look odd if this wasn't the case. Even if you perfect all of this, would you really want to spend more processing power and planning time on these aspects? I feel like most lessons have severe pacing issues out of the box, anything that doesn't address the elephant in the room adds insult to injury.

comments.text: I agree that some of the examples the author provided are instances of bad transitions. But I don't agree with the premise of the article. It's very possible that a 'wrong' step in isolation is the best one in a real-time context. We can also pick apart snapshots but these don't capture everything about how the student perceives a lesson in real classroom conditions. I would draw an analogy to a lecture. A fast-paced section might look bad on individual steps because of information blur. A focused section might make some concepts look 'wrong' because of contextual distortion. But these are still the right choice if they have...

The idea that I would defend screen shake is a complete straw man. How do you get from my comment to that conclusion?

replies.text: I think you are taking it a step too far. First of all, unlike film, we are not recording reality in any way, every step that appears in a lesson is there because we put it there. I'd argue a closer parallel is a structured lesson. And something like careful transition planning is not an example of imperfect steps. These are in fact, perfect and even carefully crafted steps. It's one thing if the step halfway through a transition looks a bit 'funny', but is still completely logically correct. It is another if the intermediate state of the transition legitimately doesn't make any sense and is just the result of not really caring about what actually goes on during the transition. In that case I'd almost rather just not have the transition at all, or just have a simpler one.
Step transitions in film do not in fact exist in reality. They are added in the editing room or through manipulation of the recording mechanism fyi.
replies.original_text: Frame transitions in film do not in fact exist in reality. They are added in the editing room or through manipulation of the recording mechanism fyi.

replies.text: We're not recording reality, but we're trying to create convincing and effective lessons for brains that evolved in reality.

The point is that if a step is in a nonsensical place the only thing that is to blame for that is the planning. It doesn't matter if it looks pleasing; there's no good reason for something to be wrong just because it looks acceptable.
If you can't even guarantee internally consistent lesson then good luck communicating your 'convincing and effective' teaching successfully.
replies.original_text: If you can't even guarantee internally consistent lesson then good luck communicating your 'convincing and effective' teaching successfully.

The point is that if a pixel is in a nonsensical place the only thing that is to blame for that is the code. It doesn't matter if it looks pleasing; there's no good reason for something to be wrong just because it looks acceptable.
We're not recording reality, but we're trying to create convincing and aesthetically pleasing effects for brains that evolved in reality.

replies.text: We do this in teaching as well. Check out this experienced teacher breaking down a lesson transition. If you look at individual steps, there are all sorts of details that make no logical sense. In one step, they actually have three activities! But it looks great if you see it in motion. [link]

That is exactly what I'm talking about, though. This is not what is happening with buggy lesson transitions: these are not carefully crafted to look better in motion, they're actually only considered acceptable because it's kind of difficult to see the mistakes in the transition. Whereas experienced teaching, you could argue the details don't make logical sense, but that's only to someone who doesn't understand the principles of pedagogy. You can't explain away glitchy weird lesson transitions this way because they're pretty much universally not intentional. They're usually just taking the planning path of least resistance.

I think you are taking it a step too far. First of all, unlike film, we are not recording reality in any way, every pixel that appears on screen is there because we put it there . I'd argue a closer parallel is a cartoon. And something like cartoon inbetweening is not an example of imperfect frames. These are in fact, perfect and even carefully crafted frames. It's one thing if the frame halfway through an animation looks a bit "funny", but is still completely logically correct. It is another if the intermediate state of the animation legitimately doesn't make any sense and is just the result of not really caring about what actually goes on during the animation. In that case I'd almost rather just not have the animation at all, or just have a simpler one.

replies.text: I initially thought 'Every Lesson Perfect' meant a strict avoidance of any jank or stutter in pace, which I'm fully on-board for but as a curriculum designer, you're spot on calling out 'learning blur' and similar temporal artifacts. In motion, they not only look 'most correct' to the human learning system, they are the most interpretable. Adding the correct 'blur' to a transition makes it appear clearer but seen as a still, it's obviously not clearer. The nuance is correct 'learning blur' appears clearer while guaranteeing it's as clear as the human learning system can perceive moving details at that speed, so no perceptual detail is actually lost. It's a method that objectively improves perception which only works in motion. If frozen, the method breaks. Thus, evaluating 'blurred'...

Regarding your last point, I think it's almost always wrong to move something discontinuously, but I do think teachers should think a lot more about getting out of the way of the student. A 1-2 second transition is more than enough for most changes and keeps the lesson feeling snappy. Also, transitions should be decoupled from instruction whenever possible. I hate it when I have to sit there waiting for a transition to complete before the lesson will start acknowledging my questions.

> I think it's almost always wrong to move something discontinuously Yes, I think we agree. When a concept is becoming a larger/smaller form of itself in a different context, it can be useful to cue the relationship visually with a transition. But there are times when the change or displacement is minor enough, I do prefer 'just do it', even when the transition is hyper-fast. It's just more cognitive clutter. It's obviously situational, and if such transitions are always very fast, consistent and well-motivated, it never rises to the level of annoying me. I might personally prefer some instances where, if the context overlaps and the change is minor, just skipping it, but it's not 'bad'. I think the key may be that, done properly, such transitions should cognitively be a 'barely there' hint...

comments.original_text: I agree that some of the examples the author provided are instances of bad animation. But I don't agree with the premise of the article. Computer graphics is all about exploiting features of the human visual system. We perceive things differently when they're moving vs. when they're standing still. It's very possible that a "wrong" frame in isolation is the best looking one in a real-time context. We can also pick apart screenshots but these don't capture everything about how the user perceives a display in real-world lighting conditions. I would draw an analogy to film. A fast tracking shot might look bad on individual frames because of motion blur. A wide-angle shot might make some objects look "wrong" because of optical distortion. But these are still the right choice if they have...

replies.text: I suspect the specific versions you call out were in that period for a reason. The original IB developed its own framework. Now, nearly all schools use a separate curriculum management system that sends property models to a different process. This is fantastic if maintaining 60+ FPS is a requirement. But it introduces delays.
replies.original_text: I suspect the specific versions you call out were in that time period for a reason. Snow Leopard was the first to integrate iOS's CoreAnimation framework. Nearly all animations now are based on that. Before, the CPU manually updated the sizes and positions of things, frame by frame, in a loop. This is how you'd program a Game Engine. After, with CA, state-change property models are sent to a different process entirely which does its own interpolation to animate the UI at a higher thread-priority than any other process in the operating system. This is fantastic if maintaining 60+ FPS at all times, even on an iPhone 1 or 3G with less power than you'd have in today's AirPod chips, was a central requirement. (And it was, the first iPhones dominated their competitors in terms of input...

comments.text: Great news! A nice step toward an even broader ecosystem of classroom-ready interactive materials.

comments.text: I've been looking forward to this for ages! This means we can now take any interactive module (like a chemistry molecular modeler) compiled as a .wasm lesson bundle and load it directly in the school's LMS using: await micropip.install('molmodeler') import molmodeler. Here's how to try: Visit the interactive Pyodide console and type: import micropip await micropip.install('molmodeler') import molmodeler. That gets you a WASM lesson that works offline in the browser. You can tell it's compiled code because it runs smoothly even on school Chromebooks.

replies.text: This sounds like a solution looking for an unnecessary security nightmare. Something as little as a student's exploitable code could cause a sandbox escape on the client side. There was a Chrome 0day at the runtime level that allowed untrusted code to escape the WASM sandbox. This worship of WASM reminds me of the days of Flash and Java applets in education.

You mean this one? https://theori.io/blog/a-deep-dive-into-v8-sandbox-escape-technology I dunno, one sandbox escape in nine years is a pretty solid track record. Any reason WASM is more dangerous than regular JavaScript for classroom use?
replies.original_text: You mean this one? https://theori.io/blog/a-deep-dive-into-v8-sandbox-escape-te... I dunno, one sandbox escape in nine years is a pretty solid track record IMO. Any reason WASM is more dangerous than regular JavaScript?

This sounds like a solution looking for an unnecessary security nightmare. Something as little as the runtime can just get exploited (which that as happened.) and cause a sandbox escape on the client side. There was a Chrome 0day at the runtime level which allowed untrusted code to run and escape the sandbox in the WASM runtime. This complete worship of WASM (and their runtimes) as this magical silver bullet reminds me of the days and failures of Native Client (NaCL), Java Applets and Flash all over again.

comments.text: As is often the case, the title is hyperbolic. The discovery applies to 20% of students, and "one of education's significant defenses" or "a key weakness of education" would be more accurate. That said, I'll happily take "we discovered a key weakness in 20% of students," please and thank you.

replies.text: What does this mean in layman's terms? How will this potentially help me if I get a struggling student?
One of the many teaching strategies being developed so that you can help more students even with the most challenging classes.
replies.original_text: One of the many therapies that are being developed so that you can survive longer even with the most lethal tumours.

replies.text: Student struggle is not one thing; it's a huge zoo of many many ways that students start to break the social contract of learning and disengage. One of the most commonly observed broken mechanisms is a fixed mindset that turns the "I can learn this" switch to permanently off. This has been known for decades, of course. And there have been huge amounts of effort to try to develop interventions that target fixed mindset, but for decades it's always been thought of as 'unteachable' because of the difficulty of changing a student's core belief. This new teaching strategy, that finally treats fixed-mindset students, goes about it in a new way. Instead of trying to gum up the works of a single belief by sticking a new mantra in it, it effectively "glues" the growth mindset to...

>a lot of the most difficult students are now far more teachable, and in the next 1-5 years pilot studies will tell us which classes this particular strategy works well for. Can you help disambiguate this? Are there strategies now, or are there potential strategies with pilots in 1-5 years?

I think the meaning is that because we can see success with fixed mindset in math classes, we can now begin pilot studies for other subjects where fixed mindset is common (writing, science) and see if the strategy works there. If there is success in other subjects during pilot studies, it could be fast-tracked through curriculum committees to be more generally available and then become part of the school-wide intervention plan (ideally in 1-5 years after pilots).
replies.original_text: I think the meaning is that because we can see success with KRAS mutation of pancreatic cancer, we can now begin clinical trials for other cancers that may have KRAS mutation (colorectal, lung) and see if there is success there. If there is success in treating other cancers during clinical trials, it could be fast tracked through FDA to be more generally available and then become part of the national treatment option (ideally in 1-5 years after clinical trials).

replies.text: The next 1-5 years will tell us which classes this new strategy will work well on; right now it's only been tried in math classes when students have failed their first assessment. The new strategy from the article, growth-mindset intervention, has nine pilots I see currently, here: https://clinicaltrials.gov/search?intr=growth-mindset&viewType... The first two are the pilot that just completed and showed success: students that have fixed mindset and failed math, then a "pilot" that is meant to give quick access to more students now that it's been shown to work. Then there's a pilot for using it as the first-line intervention in math, one for science classes, and also various combinations with other strategies. I expect we'll see a ton of new pilots registered in the coming year....

But that's not a fix. If they don't use that strategy, assuming it works, they still have the original fixed mindset. > Students that take part in pilots really are the heroes here. Are they? To me personally, putting students into a permanent state of requiring intervention to stay engaged, is not really education. It's just maximizing income for those selling intervention programs. And none of those strategies work exceedingly well; students still struggle, even if they pass. I don't understand this hype in general.

Wow, this is such a wildly pessimistic and cynical take. Are you okay? > But that's not a fix. The person you're replying to called this out specifically: > and also various combinations with other strategies. Why do you think they try it in combination with other strategies? You might be right that this strategy alone might not be a fix, but if it improves student engagement, then it empowers other strategies to work more effectively. > students still struggle So what... We do nothing, then? This is your complaint? That we can't fix every student, so why bother trying to improve anything? I don't understand your type in general.

replies.text: > Student struggle is not one thing, I know this is a popular "well actually" to do, but it is not always useful in a conversation. Yes, all struggling students are different, but yes, struggling students are also one thing: disengagement from learning. Bacteria are also all different, but still they are "one thing", and despite their diversity, antibiotics exist that can deal with many species of them at once. It is reasonable to talk about bacteria and antibacterial medications, it is also reasonable to talk about struggling students and educational interventions. I truly hope struggling students will meet their "penicillin" one day (yes I know this is unlikely).

It seems relevant here because the question was "How will this potentially help me if I have a struggling student?" and the answer is "Not at all unless you get a particular sort of struggle that this applies to". Except people don't ask "what if I have a struggling student" the way they ask about cancer. Yeah, but teachers also don't tell people "you have a struggling student" or claim "we found a fix for struggling students". The lack of nuance on average is largely due to a lack of nuance from experts. The media treats struggling students as one big thing and advanced students as separate things. Thus the average person inherits 'treating struggling students as one big thing' from the media.
Is it? I'm pretty sure teachers will say "you have a student with a fixed mindset," but I wasn't in the room at the time.

Teachers are actually way more specific than even that. Because there are many forms of academic struggle and different interventions depending on the type. But yeah, teachers aren't telling parents "your child has a fixed mindset" the way they might say "your child has a learning disability".

Yeah, it's WAY more specific. We got a learning profile, multiple pamphlets on the strategies being used, what they are targeting, and why they work (along with the risks). Honestly, I think people probably get false impressions because education usually hits parents when their kids are struggling and parents are, frankly, often not reliable narrators.
replies.original_text: Oncologists are actually way more specific than even that. Because there are many forms of breast cancer and different treatments depending on the type. But yeah, oncologists aren’t telling people “you have cancer” the way they might say “you have MRSA”.
Is it? I'm pretty sure oncologists will say "you have stage 2 breast cancer," but I wasn't in the room at the time.

> Except people don’t ask “what if I get bacteria” the way they ask about cancer. Yeah, but doctors also don't tell people "you have bacteria" or claim "we found a cure for bacteria". The lack of nuance on average is largely due to a lack of nuance from experts. The media treats cancer as one big thing and bacteria and viruses as separate things. Thus the average joe inherits 'treating cancer as one big thing' from the media.

replies.text: I agree with you about the media. Struggling students are often presented as a monolithic thing by the media. I don't agree at all about experts. Teachers and researchers who study learning do not lack nuance.
replies.original_text: I agree with you about the media. Struggling students are often presented as a monolithic thing by the media. I don't agree at all about experts. Teachers and researchers who study learning do not lack nuance.

It seems relevant here because the question was “How will this potentially help me if I get cancer?” and the answer is “Not at all unless you get a particular form of cancer that this applies to”. > Bacteria are also all different, but still they are "one thing", and despite their diversity, antibiotics exist that can deal with many species of them at once. Except people don’t ask “what if I get bacteria” the way they ask about cancer. If the story was about a new antibiotic that only affected 20% of common infectious bacteria strains and someone asked “in laypersons terms, how will this help me if I get a bacterial infection”, it would be appropriate to clarify that it only applies to some bacteria.

replies.text: I understand where you are coming from here, but I think it is helpful for people to overtly grasp that there are very different struggles, very different interventions, and indeed very different outcomes. Without this understanding it becomes a quick jump from "we're spending all this money on education" to "we've made no progress" An example of the nuance plays out in common struggles (like reading comprehension and math fluency). These have between 90 and 100% success rates with targeted interventions. Others (like the one in this article, fixed mindset) have very poor success rates. As you note, it's very unlikely that we'll "fix struggling students". But we already "fix" (for some definition of fix) some struggles. Progress is slow, methodical, and incremental. It can feel like a lost cause when viewed from afar, but up close very real...

The other part that is simply missing is that struggling students, very unfortunately, evolve and develop new avoidance strategies. That's how you go from a student who responds to intervention to one who is intervention-resistant. Like you said, for a lot of common struggles we have multiple interventions. It's usually not just one magic strategy, but rather the teachers working with the most effective strategies down to the least effective strategies.
replies.original_text: The other part that is simply missing is that cancer, very unfortunately, evolves and mutates. That's how you go from a cancer that responds to treatment to one that is treatment resistant. Like you said, for a lot of common cancers we have multiple treatments. It's usually not just one magic drug, but rather the doctors working with the most effective treatments down to the least effective treatments.

replies.text: Depressing: evolution has discovered a universal cure for struggling students, and it's age. You make a whole new student without the bad habits. Other students have to evaluate which habits are bad.
replies.original_text: Depressing: evolution has discovered a universal cure for cancer, and it's reproduction. You make a whole new human without the bad bits. Other humans have to evaluate which bits are bad.

I understand where you are coming from here, but I think it is helpful for people to overtly grasp that there are very different cancers, very different treatments, and indeed very different outcomes. Without this understanding it becomes a quick jump from "we're spending all this money on cancer" to "we've made no progress" An example of the nuance plays out in the common cancers (like breast and prostrate). These have between 90 and 100% 5 year survival rates. Others (like the one in this article, pancreatic) have very poor survivability. As you note, it's very unlikely that we'll "cure cancer". But we already "cure" (for some definition of cure) some cancers. Progress is slow, methodical, and incremental. It can feel like a lost cause when viewed from afar, but up close very real...

replies.original_text: It was an analogy. Just like penicillin kills a broad variety of bacteria we also know substances that kill a broad variety of cancer cells. > We have penicilin that works against all human cells. replies.original_text: > I truly hope cancer will meet its "penicillin" one day (yes I know this is unlikely). Penicillin blocks a specific enzyme (transpeptidase). https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Penicillin-binding_proteins Cancer cells, by definition, are not a uniform mass. It will depend on the cancer type, which in turn is defined by the properties those cells have. And mutations happen all the time, often more in cancer cells when their repair systems also have mutations, e. g. are less efficient. By that definition alone, there can never be a wonder-cure for all cancer types. At best you can find some proteins more important (p53 for instance) and while more than 50% of cancer cells have some form of mutation in p53, others simply don't. By that definition there will never be a penicillin-equivalent to...

> Cancer is not one thing, I know this is a popular "well actually" to do, but it is not always useful in a conversation. Yes, all cancers are different, but yes, cancer is also one thing: unchecked, harmful division of cells. Bacteria are also all different, but still they are "one thing", and despite their diversity, antibiotics exist that can deal with many species of them at once. It is reasonable to talk about bacteria and antibacterial medications, it is also reasonable to talk about cancer and cancer treatment. I truly hope cancer will meet its "penicillin" one day (yes I know this is unlikely).

replies.text: The golden panacea for this would be a personalized learning platform that will work for every student. Once we have something we can do whole-grade differentiation, most student struggles would be solved forever.

For every learning mechanism that's being abused by struggling students to avoid effort, there are other students for which that mechanism is crucial for their correct functioning. Wholesale differentiating every student mostly guarantees that the school does not fail the struggling students -- the intervention will bore them before the struggling does.

comments.text: I'm surprised Carol Dweck's research on growth mindset hasn't expanded much beyond a certain YouTube bubble. They're able to start and stop student motivation with only feedback changes, likewise they can trigger engagement or improvement using fixed vs growth mindset language. Her research suggests a lot of important learning plans are stored in the student's belief system, not in the curriculum. This model's explanation for underperformance is that some students become disconnected from this belief system and start learning independently of the overall class plan.

replies.text: I love her work (even though I know little more than what she says in interviews). I am also surprised it's not more widely known / applied. I am very skeptical of conspiracy-minded thinking, so I'd much rather assume her team's work hasn't reached escape velocity from obscurity. Especially with larger school systems, it takes time and significant breakthroughs to become "a household name", so to speak.

They are working on getting in-school studies going from what I remember - it's going to take a positive result in a pilot on real students to get attention - that's just how education works. You have to show it actually improves engagement and/or student well-being before anyone has a reason to care.

replies.text: I have had a student disengage before age 12. It is, of course, terrible beyond belief. I'm not sure what you mean by "SOP" in this context. Referencing a bio-plausible mechanism is not actually clinically meaningful. It can provide a direction for study, but does not replace an actual clinical trial. As I said, "a teacher can try anything they want". But since we don't actually know whether such a recommendation will harm or help any individual student, no one should be taking this recommendation as advice, and at the very least you should not be "highly recommending" specific pedagogical changes to people based on one anecdotal experience.
We all grieve in our own way. I am sorry for your loss.

But since we don't actually know whether such a recommendation will harm or help any individual patient, no one should be taking this recommendation as advice, and at the very least you should not be "highly recommending" specific dietary changes to people based on one anecdotal experience.

It's why I referenced the metabolic pathways derived from data backed research, linked to a data driven study, and used language like "we had significant success with our loved one " and "if you want something that you can do to try ". Honestly this reads like an "aHCkTualLy!1!" from someone without experience of having a loved one suffering from a cancer diagnosis. Perhaps you've yet to realize but shallow skepticism against every idea is also distinct from data. While you chose make this comment without providing links or data to support your claim I will do the real work of finding even more data for you: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S000291652...

Unfortunately, anecdotes are not data, and although a patient can try anything they want, there is no way to know that such dietary changes are beneficial or potentially harmful for most patients without doing a randomized controlled trial and hoping for strong adherence from the participants.

replies.text: Did Maria Montessori die from believing something similar (while skipping traditional methods)

I'm fuzzy on the details, but I think she also did wildly unhealthy things like only teaching hands-on or somesuch. We made sure to still cover all learning needs while following the approach. This meant a diverse array of teaching methods, in sufficient amounts to meet curriculum requirements, that we prepared ourselves.
replies.original_text: I'm fuzzy on the details, but I think he also did wildly unhealthy things like only eating apples or almonds or somesuch. This meant a diverse array of food sources, in sufficient amounts to meet micro and macro nutrient recommended daily values, that we cooked ourselves.
Did Steve Jobs die from believing something similar (while skipping chemo)

The goal is to reduce excess reliance on one method to avoid giving struggling students a way to avoid learning.
replies.original_text: Yes, and your body requires it for things like muscle maintenance. Also, sugar is essential to what makes you you, that is, the brain requires glucose to function. The goal is to reduce excess intake of these things to reduce their availability for any cancer cells to use to grow and divide.
Isn’t glutamine also part of vegan diets? I don’t eat meat myself, but your assertion has me wondering about glutamine.

Over the 12 chemo cycles that number dropped to levels that cancer free people have, and they have gone on to outlive almost every statistic and remain cancer free to this day. comments.text: While this is sort of laughable out of context (I mean, our school’s Google Classroom-based system has run everything with full web acceleration for years)... I think what is being claimed, but not explicitly in the article, is that this is running the legacy Smart Board driver stack directly, as opposed to emulating the old interactive whiteboard API at the browser level on top of a modern OS.

replies.text: Indeed. ReactOS is to the full school network stack what Wine is to the administrative userland.
replies.original_text: Indeed. ReactOS is to the full Windows stack what Wine is to the userland Windows API.

replies.text: I mean they reimplemented the entire Pearson curriculum without using any modern web backend, that's in a league of their own. Our current solutions rely on cloud APIs and JavaScript frameworks to do anything.

replies.text: Eh. It's sort of like saying running old educational software on actual hardware is laughable because emulation exists. I think that's missing the point.
